topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: Demonstrate how to access aviation meteorological information for New Zealand through the MetFlight internet web-site.
concept: MetFlight is your go-to source for aviation weather in New Zealand. It's the official MetService aviation weather portal, and you'll access it at metflight.metra.co.nz. You can pull up METARs, TAFs, ARFORs, SIGMETs, satellite imagery, and rain radar  -  basically everything you need to make a go/no-go decision. You'll need a login (your flight school will sort that out), and it's worth getting comfortable navigating it early because you'll use it before every single flight.
exam_tip: Know that MetFlight is the official NZ aviation weather portal provided by MetService. You won't be asked to navigate it in the exam, but you need to know it exists and what it provides.
key_facts: MetFlight is the official NZ aviation weather portal
Provided by MetService New Zealand
Accessed via metflight.metra.co.nz
Provides METARs, TAFs, ARFORs, SIGMETs, satellite and radar imagery
Requires a registered login
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, MetService NZ

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in an ARFOR.
concept: An ARFOR (Area Forecast) gives you the big picture of expected weather over a large area. In NZ, ARFORs cover defined geographical areas and describe the general weather, cloud, visibility, wind, icing, and turbulence expected during the validity period. They're issued in plain language with coded elements, and they're your starting point for understanding what the weather is going to do across your route. Think of it as the weather story for the whole region  -  you then zoom in with TAFs and METARs for specific aerodromes.
exam_tip: ARFORs cover large areas and give the big picture. Know how to interpret cloud layers (BKN, OVC, SCT, FEW), wind at altitude, and any embedded hazards like icing or turbulence levels.
key_facts: ARFOR = Area Forecast  -  covers a defined geographical region
Provides general weather, cloud, visibility, winds, icing, and turbulence
Issued by MetService NZ for defined NZ areas
Valid for a specified time period (typically 12-24 hours)
Your starting point for route weather planning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ MET section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in a TAF.
concept: A TAF (Terminal Aerodrome Forecast) is a forecast of weather conditions expected at a specific aerodrome. In NZ, TAFs are issued for the main aerodromes and cover a period of either 9, 18, or 24 hours depending on the aerodrome. They use a coded format similar to METARs and include expected surface wind, visibility, weather phenomena, and cloud. TAFs use change groups like BECMG (becoming), TEMPO (temporary), and PROB30/PROB40 to show how conditions are expected to evolve. If you're planning a flight to an aerodrome, the TAF tells you what to expect when you get there.
exam_tip: Know the difference between BECMG (permanent change over a period), TEMPO (temporary fluctuations, each lasting less than an hour), and PROB30/PROB40 (probability). TAF validity periods vary by aerodrome.
key_facts: TAF = Terminal Aerodrome Forecast for a specific aerodrome
Covers 9, 18, or 24 hours depending on the aerodrome
Coded format: wind, visibility, weather, cloud
BECMG = permanent change over a time window
TEMPO = temporary fluctuations, each lasting less than 1 hour
PROB30/PROB40 = probability of occurrence
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ MET section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in a TREND.
concept: A TREND is a short-range forecast appended to the end of a METAR or SPECI. It covers the next 2 hours from the time of the observation and tells you how conditions are expected to change (or not) in the near term. If no significant change is expected, you'll see NOSIG (no significant change). Otherwise, it uses BECMG or TEMPO groups just like a TAF. TRENDs are particularly useful when you're about to depart or arrive  -  they're the freshest forecast you'll get for that aerodrome.
exam_tip: TREND covers 2 hours from the METAR time. NOSIG means no significant change expected. It's appended to METARs and SPECIs at selected NZ aerodromes.
key_facts: TREND = short-range forecast appended to a METAR or SPECI
Covers the next 2 hours from observation time
NOSIG = no significant change expected
Uses BECMG and TEMPO change groups
Only issued at selected aerodromes in NZ
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ MET section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in a METAR.
concept: A METAR (Meteorological Aerodrome Report) is an actual observation of current weather conditions at an aerodrome, taken at regular intervals  -  typically every 30 minutes at major NZ aerodromes. It gives you the current wind, visibility, weather phenomena, cloud, temperature, dew point, and QNH. Reading a METAR is a core skill  -  you need to know the format cold. The order is always: identification, time, wind, visibility, weather, cloud, temperature/dew point, QNH. Wind is given in degrees true and knots.
exam_tip: METAR wind is always in degrees TRUE. Temperature and dew point are in whole degrees Celsius. QNH is in hectopascals. Cloud heights are in feet above aerodrome level (AAL). Know the cloud amount codes: FEW (1-2 oktas), SCT (3-4), BKN (5-7), OVC (8).
key_facts: METAR = actual weather observation at an aerodrome
Issued typically every 30 minutes at main NZ aerodromes
Format order: ID, time, wind, visibility, weather, cloud, temp/dewpoint, QNH
Wind direction in degrees TRUE, speed in knots
Cloud amount: FEW (1-2 oktas), SCT (3-4), BKN (5-7), OVC (8)
Temperature and dew point in whole °C
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ MET section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in a SPECI.
concept: A SPECI is a special weather observation issued between routine METARs when conditions change significantly. If the weather deteriorates or improves past certain thresholds  -  say visibility drops below 5000 m, wind shifts significantly, thunderstorms develop, or cloud base drops below key levels  -  a SPECI is triggered. It uses exactly the same format as a METAR, and you decode it the same way. The key difference is timing: a METAR comes at regular intervals, while a SPECI is event-driven. If you see a SPECI for your destination, pay attention  -  something has changed.
exam_tip: SPECI = special observation triggered by significant weather changes. Same format as a METAR. If you see one, conditions have changed enough to warrant an unscheduled report  -  take notice.
key_facts: SPECI = special weather observation between routine METARs
Triggered by significant changes in conditions
Same coded format as a METAR
Triggers include: wind shifts, visibility changes, thunderstorms, cloud base changes
Always indicates something noteworthy has changed
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ MET section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in a METAR AUTO.
concept: A METAR AUTO is an automated weather observation generated by an Automatic Weather Station (AWS) without any human input. You'll see these at many NZ aerodromes, especially smaller ones that don't have a human observer on site. The key difference from a regular METAR is that the cloud and visibility data have limitations  -  the AWS can only sense cloud directly overhead (not on the horizon) and visibility is measured at a single point. It also can't identify specific weather phenomena like rain type as reliably. That's why some fields may show '///'  -  the sensor can't determine that element.
exam_tip: METAR AUTO = no human observer involved. Cloud sensor only detects cloud directly above (narrow beam). Visibility measured at a single point. '///' means the sensor can't determine that element. Be cautious with AUTO reports  -  they have real limitations.
key_facts: METAR AUTO = fully automated observation from an AWS
No human observer involvement
Cloud sensor only detects cloud in a narrow beam overhead
Visibility measured at a single point, not representative of all directions
'///' in a field means the sensor cannot determine that parameter
Common at smaller NZ aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ MET section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in a SIGMET.
concept: A SIGMET (Significant Meteorological Information) is a warning of hazardous weather that could affect the safety of all aircraft. In NZ, SIGMETs are issued for conditions like severe turbulence, severe icing, thunderstorms, volcanic ash, and mountain waves. They cover a defined area and altitude range, and they're valid for a limited period (usually up to 4 hours). If there's a SIGMET active along your route, you need to take it very seriously  -  these warnings exist because the conditions described can be dangerous even to experienced pilots in larger aircraft.
exam_tip: SIGMETs warn of weather hazardous to ALL aircraft  -  severe turbulence, severe icing, thunderstorms, volcanic ash, mountain waves. Valid up to 4 hours. Don't fly into SIGMET conditions in a light aircraft.
key_facts: SIGMET = warning of hazardous weather for ALL aircraft
Covers severe turbulence, severe icing, thunderstorms, volcanic ash, mountain waves
Issued for a defined area and altitude range
Typically valid for up to 4 hours
Issued by MetService NZ
Must be taken seriously  -  describes conditions dangerous to all aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ MET section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in an ATIS.
concept: ATIS (Automatic Terminal Information Service) is a continuous broadcast on a dedicated frequency at busier NZ aerodromes like Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch, and Queenstown. It gives you the current weather, runway in use, approach type, and any NOTAMs affecting the aerodrome. Each ATIS broadcast is assigned a letter (Alpha, Bravo, Charlie, etc.) so ATC knows which version you've received. You acknowledge the current ATIS letter in your initial call. ATIS is updated whenever conditions change significantly, or at least every 30 minutes.
exam_tip: ATIS broadcasts include weather, runway, and NOTAMs. Each update gets a new letter identifier. You must state the current ATIS letter on initial contact with ATC. Updated at least every 30 minutes or when conditions change.
key_facts: ATIS = continuous broadcast of aerodrome information
Available at busier NZ aerodromes (Auckland, Wellington, Christchurch, Queenstown, etc.)
Includes weather, runway in use, approach type, relevant NOTAMs
Each update assigned a sequential letter identifier
Updated at least every 30 minutes or when conditions change
Pilot acknowledges current letter on initial ATC contact
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ AD section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in an AWIB.
concept: An AWIB (Aerodrome Weather Information Broadcast) is the weather information service at non-towered aerodromes in NZ. Unlike ATIS (which is at controlled aerodromes), AWIB provides automated weather information generated by an Automatic Weather Station. You access it on the published frequency, and it typically gives you wind, QNH, temperature, dew point, and any available cloud information. The data is automated, so it has the same limitations as METAR AUTO  -  the cloud sensor only looks straight up, and the visibility sensor measures at a single point.
exam_tip: AWIB is for non-towered aerodromes; ATIS is for towered ones. AWIB data is automated (AWS) so has the same limitations as METAR AUTO. Check AIPNZ for which aerodromes have AWIB.
key_facts: AWIB = automated weather broadcast at non-towered aerodromes
Generated by Automatic Weather Station (AWS)
Provides wind, QNH, temperature, dew point, cloud info
Same sensor limitations as METAR AUTO
Accessed on published aerodrome frequency
Different from ATIS which is at controlled aerodromes
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ AD section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in a BWR.
concept: A BWR (Basic Weather Report) is a weather observation from a non-specialist observer  -  often a Flight Service operator or other trained person at an aerodrome. It's simpler than a METAR and uses a less formal format, but it gives you useful ground-truth information about conditions. BWRs typically include wind direction and speed, visibility, cloud cover and base, temperature, and QNH. You'll find these at smaller NZ aerodromes that don't have automated or professional meteorological observations. They're valuable because they come from a human who can look around and describe what's actually happening.
exam_tip: BWR = observation from a trained non-specialist observer. Less formal than a METAR but comes from a human observer, which can be more useful than AWS data for some elements like visibility and weather phenomena.
key_facts: BWR = Basic Weather Report from a trained non-specialist observer
Simpler format than a METAR
Typically includes wind, visibility, cloud, temperature, QNH
Available at smaller NZ aerodromes
Human observer can report elements AWS sensors may miss
Valuable ground-truth information
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ MET section

topic: 8.2  -  Decode Domestic Met Reports and Forecasts
syllabus_desc: In plain language, decode the information contained in Pilot Reports.
concept: Pilot reports (PIREPs) are weather observations made by pilots in flight and relayed to ATC or Flight Information Service. They're incredibly valuable because they provide real-time, first-hand information about conditions aloft  -  things like actual turbulence intensity, icing levels, cloud tops and bases, and visibility. In NZ, if you encounter significant weather, you're encouraged to pass a pilot report. These reports help other pilots and forecasters. When you receive one from ATC, pay attention  -  it's coming from someone who's actually flying through those conditions right now.
exam_tip: Pilot reports are real-time observations from pilots in flight  -  they're the most current information about conditions aloft. You should both use them in your planning and contribute them when you encounter significant weather.
key_facts: Pilot reports (PIREPs) are weather observations from pilots in flight
Provide real-time info on turbulence, icing, cloud tops/bases, visibility
Relayed via ATC or Flight Information Service
Pilots are encouraged to report significant weather conditions
Most current source of information about conditions aloft
Help both other pilots and meteorological forecasters
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR section

syllabus_desc: Identify features found on surface weather maps including isobars, anticyclones, depressions, ridges, troughs, cols, fronts, and tropical cyclones.
concept: Surface weather maps are your big-picture tool for understanding what's driving the weather. The key features you need to identify are: isobars (lines of equal pressure  -  the closer together, the stronger the wind), anticyclones (highs  -  clockwise rotation in the Southern Hemisphere, generally settled weather), depressions or lows (anticlockwise rotation in the SH, often unsettled weather), ridges (extensions of high pressure), troughs (extensions of low pressure  -  often trigger weather changes), cols (neutral areas between systems), fronts (cold, warm, occluded, and stationary boundaries between air masses), and tropical cyclones (intense lows from the tropics that occasionally affect northern NZ).
exam_tip: In the Southern Hemisphere, wind flows clockwise around highs and anticlockwise around lows. Closely spaced isobars = strong winds. Troughs often mark weather changes and are associated with fronts.
key_facts: Isobars = lines of equal pressure; close spacing means strong winds
Anticyclone (high) = clockwise flow in SH, generally settled weather
Depression (low) = anticlockwise flow in SH, unsettled weather
Ridge = extension of high pressure
Trough = extension of low pressure, often with weather changes
Col = neutral area between pressure systems

syllabus_desc: Explain the most common weather characteristics of each feature.
concept: Each pressure feature brings its own typical weather. Anticyclones (highs) generally mean light winds, clear skies, and stable conditions  -  but they can trap pollution and moisture under inversions, leading to fog and haze. Depressions (lows) typically bring cloud, rain, and stronger winds. Ridges act like temporary highs  -  a break between systems with clearing conditions. Troughs are the troublemakers  -  they're where wind shifts happen and they often contain fronts with cloud, rain, and gusty wind changes. Cols are where nothing much is driving the weather, so conditions tend to be light and variable  -  but fog can form easily. Fronts bring their own distinct weather patterns depending on type.
exam_tip: Anticyclones = settled but can trap fog/haze. Depressions = unsettled, cloud, rain, wind. Troughs = wind shifts and frontal weather. Cols = light winds, variable, fog risk.
key_facts: Anticyclones: light winds, clear skies, inversions possible, fog risk
Depressions: cloud, rain, stronger winds, unsettled
Ridges: temporary clearing between systems
Troughs: wind shifts, frontal weather, turbulence
Cols: light variable winds, fog risk in moist conditions
Tropical cyclones: rare in NZ but bring extreme conditions when they reach us

syllabus_desc: Define pressure gradient.
concept: Pressure gradient is the rate of change of atmospheric pressure over a horizontal distance. On a weather map, you can see it by looking at how closely spaced the isobars are. The closer together the isobars, the steeper the pressure gradient, and the stronger the wind. It's exactly like contour lines on a topographic map  -  close contours mean a steep slope. So when you look at a weather map and see isobars bunched tightly together, expect strong winds in that area. Widely spaced isobars mean a gentle pressure gradient and light winds.
exam_tip: Pressure gradient = rate of pressure change over distance. Close isobars = steep gradient = strong winds. Wide isobars = gentle gradient = light winds. Think of it like contour lines on a hill  -  steeper slope means faster flow.
key_facts: Pressure gradient = rate of pressure change over horizontal distance
Shown on weather maps by isobar spacing
Close isobars = steep gradient = strong winds
Wide isobars = gentle gradient = light winds
The pressure gradient force drives the initial air movement

syllabus_desc: Identify areas of light, moderate and strong winds on a weather map.
concept: Reading wind strength from a weather map is straightforward once you understand isobar spacing. Widely spaced isobars indicate light winds  -  you'll typically find this around the centres of anticyclones and in cols. Moderate winds appear where isobars have medium spacing, often around the edges of high pressure systems. Strong winds show up where isobars are tightly packed together  -  this is common around deep low pressure systems, in troughs, and where fronts are particularly active. In NZ, you'll often see tight isobar spacing across Cook Strait and around the bottom of the South Island when a deep low passes to the south.
exam_tip: Wide isobar spacing = light winds. Medium spacing = moderate winds. Tight spacing = strong winds. Look at the map like a contour map  -  the 'steepest' areas have the strongest wind flow.
key_facts: Wide isobar spacing = light winds (centres of highs, cols)
Medium isobar spacing = moderate winds
Tight isobar spacing = strong winds (deep lows, active troughs)
NZ frequently sees strong winds around Cook Strait with tight isobars
Wind strength is proportional to the pressure gradient

syllabus_desc: Describe the structure of the troposphere and lower stratosphere.
concept: The atmosphere has layers, and for a pilot the two that matter most are the troposphere and the lower stratosphere. The troposphere is where all the weather happens  -  it extends from the surface up to the tropopause, which sits at about 8-10 km (roughly 26,000-33,000 ft) over NZ. Temperature decreases with altitude in the troposphere, which is what drives convection and weather. Above the tropopause is the stratosphere, where temperature stops decreasing and begins to increase slightly with altitude. This temperature inversion at the tropopause acts like a lid, capping weather below it. Almost all the flying you'll do as a PPL happens well within the troposphere.
exam_tip: Troposphere = where weather happens, temperature decreases with altitude. Tropopause height varies  -  lower at the poles (~8 km), higher at the equator (~16 km), roughly 10 km over NZ. Stratosphere = stable, temperature increases with altitude.
key_facts: Troposphere: surface to tropopause, all weather occurs here
Temperature generally decreases with altitude in the troposphere
Tropopause over NZ is approximately 10 km (33,000 ft)
Tropopause is lower at the poles (~8 km) and higher at the equator (~16 km)
Stratosphere: above the tropopause, temperature increases with altitude
The tropopause acts as a lid, capping weather below

syllabus_desc: Outline the characteristics of the troposphere in terms of horizontal and vertical motions, vertical variation of density, vertical variation of temperature, and depth.
concept: The troposphere has four key characteristics you need to know. First, it's where both horizontal motions (wind) and vertical motions (convection, thermals, downdrafts) occur  -  this mixing is what creates weather. Second, air density decreases with altitude because there's less air pressing down from above. Third, temperature generally decreases with altitude at an average rate of about 2°C per 1,000 ft (the standard lapse rate)  -  though this varies a lot in practice. Fourth, the depth of the troposphere varies with latitude and season: it's shallower at the poles and deeper at the equator. Over NZ, it's about 10 km deep, varying with season.
exam_tip: Average temperature lapse rate in the troposphere is about 2°C per 1,000 ft (or 6.5°C per 1,000 m). Air density and pressure both decrease with altitude. The troposphere is deeper in summer than in winter.
key_facts: Both horizontal (wind) and vertical (convection) motions occur in the troposphere
Density decreases with increasing altitude
Temperature decreases with altitude at ~2°C per 1,000 ft on average
Troposphere depth: ~8 km at poles, ~16 km at equator, ~10 km over NZ
Depth varies with latitude and season
Mixing of air masses creates weather

syllabus_desc: List the percentages of gases in the troposphere: nitrogen, oxygen, and all other trace gases combined.
concept: The air you breathe  -  and fly through  -  is a mixture of gases that stays remarkably consistent throughout the troposphere. Nitrogen makes up about 78% and oxygen about 21%, with all the other trace gases (argon, carbon dioxide, neon, helium, etc.) making up the remaining 1%. These proportions stay essentially the same from the surface right up to about 80 km. What does change with altitude is the density  -  there are fewer molecules per cubic metre the higher you go, which is why you need supplemental oxygen above certain altitudes even though the percentage of oxygen stays the same.
exam_tip: Nitrogen ~78%, Oxygen ~21%, Other trace gases ~1%. These proportions are constant through the troposphere. The percentage of oxygen doesn't decrease with altitude  -  the density does.
key_facts: Nitrogen: approximately 78%
Oxygen: approximately 21%
All other trace gases combined: approximately 1%
Proportions remain constant throughout the troposphere
Air density (not oxygen percentage) decreases with altitude

syllabus_desc: Describe the presence and importance of water vapour and aerosols in the atmosphere.
concept: Water vapour and aerosols are the wildcards in the atmosphere. Water vapour is invisible, varies hugely from almost zero over deserts to about 4% in tropical regions, and it's the raw ingredient for all cloud, fog, rain, and icing. Despite being a tiny fraction of the atmosphere, it's the single most important gas for weather. When water vapour condenses, it releases latent heat, which fuels convection and storm development. Aerosols  -  tiny particles like dust, salt spray, pollen, and pollution  -  are important because they serve as condensation nuclei. Without them, water vapour would struggle to condense into cloud droplets. NZ's clean maritime air generally has fewer aerosols than continental locations.
exam_tip: Water vapour content varies (0-4%) and is the key ingredient for weather. Aerosols act as condensation nuclei  -  without them, clouds wouldn't form easily. NZ's maritime air is relatively clean (fewer aerosols).
key_facts: Water vapour: variable (0-4%), essential for cloud, fog, rain, icing
Water vapour is invisible  -  you can't see it, only see the results when it condenses
Condensation releases latent heat, fuelling convection and storms
Aerosols = tiny particles (dust, salt, pollen, pollution)
Aerosols serve as condensation nuclei for cloud formation
NZ maritime air generally has fewer aerosols than continental air

syllabus_desc: Outline the measurement of surface air temperature in New Zealand (as reported in aviation observations), and relate that to actual temperatures experienced above a sealed or grass runway.
concept: In NZ, surface air temperature for aviation purposes is measured in a Stevenson screen  -  a white louvred box mounted 1.2 metres above short grass. This standardised setup gives consistent, comparable readings. But here's the thing: the actual temperature you experience on a sealed (tarmac) runway on a hot day can be significantly higher than the reported temperature, because dark tarmac absorbs and radiates heat far more effectively than grass. On a summer afternoon at Ardmore or Hamilton, the air just above the sealed runway could easily be 5-10°C warmer than the official reported temperature. This matters for your takeoff performance calculations.
exam_tip: Aviation temperature is measured in a Stevenson screen at 1.2 m above grass. Actual temperatures above sealed runways can be significantly higher, especially on hot days. This affects density altitude and takeoff performance.
key_facts: Surface temperature measured in a Stevenson screen at 1.2 m above short grass
Standardised measurement ensures consistent comparisons
Sealed (tarmac) runways radiate more heat than grass surfaces
Air temperature above sealed runway can be 5-10°C higher than reported
Higher actual temperatures affect aircraft performance

syllabus_desc: Define solar and terrestrial radiation.
concept: There are two types of radiation that matter for understanding temperature. Solar radiation (also called insolation) is the energy from the sun  -  it's short-wave radiation that passes relatively easily through the atmosphere and heats the Earth's surface. Terrestrial radiation is the heat the Earth re-radiates back  -  it's long-wave radiation that's much more easily absorbed by the atmosphere (especially by water vapour and CO2). So the atmosphere is mainly heated from below by the Earth, not directly by the sun. That's why temperature generally decreases with altitude  -  you're moving away from the heat source.
exam_tip: Solar radiation = short-wave, passes through the atmosphere, heats the Earth's surface. Terrestrial radiation = long-wave, heats the atmosphere from below. This is why the atmosphere is warmest near the surface.
key_facts: Solar radiation: short-wave energy from the sun
Passes relatively easily through the atmosphere to heat the surface
Terrestrial radiation: long-wave energy re-radiated by the Earth
Easily absorbed by the atmosphere (water vapour, CO2)
Atmosphere heated mainly from below, not directly by the sun
This explains why temperature decreases with altitude

syllabus_desc: Outline the balance of incoming solar radiation versus outgoing terrestrial radiation.
concept: Over time, the Earth maintains a rough energy balance  -  the total incoming solar radiation approximately equals the total outgoing terrestrial radiation. If it didn't balance, the Earth would keep getting hotter or cooler indefinitely. During the day, incoming solar radiation exceeds outgoing terrestrial radiation, so the surface heats up. At night, there's no incoming solar radiation but the Earth keeps radiating, so the surface cools. The maximum surface temperature typically occurs in the early to mid-afternoon (a couple of hours after the sun is highest), and the minimum temperature occurs around sunrise. Understanding this cycle helps you predict conditions for your flights.
exam_tip: Max temperature: early to mid-afternoon (not at noon  -  there's a lag). Min temperature: around sunrise. During the day, incoming exceeds outgoing. At night, only outgoing radiation occurs.
key_facts: Earth maintains an overall radiation balance (incoming ≈ outgoing)
Daytime: incoming solar exceeds outgoing terrestrial  -  surface heats
Night-time: only outgoing terrestrial radiation  -  surface cools
Maximum temperature: early to mid-afternoon (not at solar noon)
Minimum temperature: around sunrise
There is a time lag between maximum solar heating and maximum temperature

syllabus_desc: Explain the effect of solar and terrestrial radiation on the daily temperature range.
concept: The daily temperature range  -  the difference between the day's maximum and minimum  -  is driven by the balance between solar heating during the day and terrestrial cooling at night. Clear skies make this range bigger: the sun heats the surface strongly during the day, and at night the heat escapes easily into space. Cloud cover reduces the range: it blocks some solar heating during the day and traps terrestrial radiation at night (like a blanket). Wind also reduces the range by mixing the air. In NZ's inland areas like the Canterbury Plains, you'll see bigger daily temperature ranges than coastal places like Wellington, because the maritime influence moderates coastal temperatures.
exam_tip: Clear skies = larger daily temperature range. Cloud cover = smaller range (blocks daytime heating, traps night-time radiation). Coastal = smaller range. Inland = bigger range. Wind mixing also reduces the range.
key_facts: Daily temperature range = max temperature minus min temperature
Clear skies increase the daily range
Cloud cover reduces the daily range (blocks solar, traps terrestrial)
Inland locations have larger daily ranges than coastal ones
Strong wind reduces the daily range through mixing
Canterbury Plains: large daily range. Wellington coast: small range

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of the following on daily air temperature: latitude, season, strong winds, wind direction, cloud cover, coastal or inland location, surface type.
concept: Several factors influence what the temperature does at any given NZ location. Latitude: NZ spans about 34°S to 47°S, so Kaitaia is warmer than Invercargill. Season: summer brings more solar heating hours and higher sun angles. Strong winds mix the air and moderate temperature extremes. Wind direction: a northerly brings warmer subtropical air to NZ, while a southerly brings cold polar air. Cloud cover acts like a blanket  -  reducing heating during the day and reducing cooling at night. Coastal locations are moderated by the sea (smaller temperature range), while inland locations have greater extremes. Surface type matters: tarmac and rock absorb and re-radiate heat quickly; grass and soil are slower; water has a very high heat capacity and changes temperature slowly.
exam_tip: Know all seven factors. In NZ: northerly = warm, southerly = cold. Coastal = moderate temperatures. Inland = temperature extremes. Cloud and wind both moderate temperatures.
key_facts: Latitude: lower = warmer (Kaitaia warmer than Invercargill)
Season: summer = higher sun angle and longer days = warmer
Strong winds mix the air, reducing temperature extremes
Wind direction: northerly = warm subtropical air, southerly = cold polar air
Cloud cover moderates temperatures day and night
Coastal locations moderated by sea; inland locations have bigger ranges
Dark sealed surfaces heat faster than grass or water

syllabus_desc: Describe the transfer of heat in the atmosphere with reference to conduction, convection, and advection.
concept: Heat gets moved around the atmosphere in three ways. Conduction is direct contact transfer  -  the ground heats the air immediately touching it, molecule by molecule. It only works in a very thin layer right at the surface (the first few centimetres), so it's not a major player for transferring heat through the atmosphere. Convection is vertical heat transfer  -  heated air near the surface rises because it's less dense, carrying heat upward. This creates thermals, cumulus clouds, and in extreme cases, thunderstorms. Advection is horizontal heat transfer  -  wind blowing warm or cold air from one place to another. When a warm northerly blows over NZ, that's advection bringing subtropical warmth. When a cold southerly hits, that's advection of polar air.
exam_tip: Conduction = direct contact (thin surface layer only). Convection = vertical transfer (rising/sinking air). Advection = horizontal transfer (wind moving air masses). Convection and advection are the main movers of heat in the atmosphere.
key_facts: Conduction: direct contact heat transfer, thin surface layer only
Convection: vertical transfer via rising and sinking air parcels
Advection: horizontal transfer of heat by wind
Convection creates thermals, cumulus clouds, and thunderstorms
Advection brings warm or cold air masses into NZ
Convection and advection are the main atmospheric heat transfer methods

syllabus_desc: Define 'atmospheric pressure'.
concept: Atmospheric pressure is simply the weight of the column of air above a given point, pressing down due to gravity. At sea level, this column extends all the way to the edge of the atmosphere. As you climb, there's less air above you, so the pressure drops. At sea level, standard atmospheric pressure is 1013.25 hPa (hectopascals). Pressure decreases roughly exponentially with altitude  -  it drops faster at low levels than at high levels. This concept is absolutely fundamental to flying because your altimeter works by measuring atmospheric pressure and converting it to an altitude reading.
exam_tip: Atmospheric pressure = weight of the air column above a point. Standard sea level pressure = 1013.25 hPa. Pressure decreases with altitude. Your altimeter is really just a pressure gauge.
key_facts: Atmospheric pressure = weight of the air column above a point
Caused by gravity acting on air molecules
Decreases with increasing altitude
Standard sea level pressure = 1013.25 hPa
Pressure decreases faster at lower altitudes than higher
Altimeter measures pressure and converts it to altitude

syllabus_desc: State the pressure units used in New Zealand aviation.
concept: In New Zealand aviation, pressure is measured in hectopascals (hPa). You'll see this on your altimeter subscale, in METARs, TAFs, and all NZ weather products. One hectopascal equals one millibar (mb), so if you see either unit, they're the same thing. Some older pilots and textbooks still use 'millibars,' but the official unit in NZ is hectopascals. Other countries use different units  -  the USA uses inches of mercury (inHg), for example  -  but in NZ, it's hPa across the board.
exam_tip: NZ uses hectopascals (hPa). 1 hPa = 1 millibar (mb). Standard sea level pressure = 1013.25 hPa. Don't confuse with inches of mercury (inHg) used in the USA.
key_facts: New Zealand uses hectopascals (hPa) for aviation pressure
1 hPa = 1 millibar (mb)
Standard sea level pressure = 1013.25 hPa
USA uses inches of mercury (inHg)  -  not used in NZ
hPa is used on altimeter subscales, METARs, TAFs, and all NZ weather products
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ GEN

syllabus_desc: State the significance of air pressure to aviation.
concept: Air pressure affects almost everything about your flight. Your altimeter uses pressure to determine altitude  -  get the pressure setting wrong and you'll be at the wrong height. Aircraft performance depends on air density, which is directly related to pressure  -  lower pressure means lower density, which means less lift, less engine power, and longer takeoff rolls. Air pressure patterns drive wind, create weather systems, and determine frontal movements. In short, pressure is the backbone of both your flight instruments and the weather itself. That's why every pre-flight briefing starts with the QNH.
exam_tip: Pressure affects: altimeter accuracy, aircraft performance (lift, power, takeoff roll), and weather patterns. Low pressure = reduced performance. Always set the correct QNH before flight.
key_facts: Altimeter accuracy depends on correct pressure settings
Aircraft performance decreases with lower pressure (less air density)
Lower pressure = less lift, less engine power, longer takeoff rolls
Pressure patterns drive wind and weather systems
QNH (pressure at sea level) is fundamental to safe flying
Pressure is the backbone of altimetry and weather prediction

syllabus_desc: Define 'pressure lapse rate'.
concept: Pressure lapse rate is the rate at which atmospheric pressure decreases with altitude. Unlike the temperature lapse rate (which is roughly constant), the pressure lapse rate is not uniform  -  pressure drops faster near the surface and more slowly at higher altitudes. This makes sense if you think about it: the air near the surface is compressed by all the air above it, so it's denser and the pressure changes faster. A useful approximation for low-level flying: pressure drops by about 1 hPa per 30 feet of altitude gained near sea level.
exam_tip: Pressure lapse rate = rate of pressure decrease with altitude. NOT constant  -  decreases faster near the surface. Rule of thumb: ~1 hPa per 30 ft near sea level.
key_facts: Pressure lapse rate = rate of pressure decrease with altitude
Not constant  -  decreases faster at lower altitudes
Near sea level: approximately 1 hPa per 30 feet
Rate slows with increasing altitude
Different from temperature lapse rate

syllabus_desc: State the approximate pressure lapse rate in the atmosphere below 10,000 ft.
concept: Below 10,000 ft, pressure drops at approximately 1 hPa per 30 feet of altitude gained. This is a handy rule of thumb that you'll use constantly. If you climb from sea level to 3,000 ft, you'd expect the pressure to drop by about 100 hPa (from, say, 1013 hPa to about 913 hPa). This approximation works well for the altitudes you'll typically be flying at PPL level. Above 10,000 ft, the rate slows because the air is less dense, but for most of your flying, the 1 hPa per 30 ft rule is your friend.
exam_tip: Below 10,000 ft: approximately 1 hPa per 30 feet. This is the most commonly tested pressure lapse rate figure. Remember it. It slows above 10,000 ft.
key_facts: Below 10,000 ft: approximately 1 hPa per 30 feet
Rule of thumb used extensively in aviation
3,000 ft climb ≈ 100 hPa pressure drop
Rate slows above 10,000 ft
Useful for crosschecking altimeter readings

syllabus_desc: Explain how surface pressure rises when air is added to the vertical column above the ground, and vice versa.
concept: Surface pressure is simply the weight of the air column above a point. If air flows into an area and piles up (convergence aloft), the column gets heavier and surface pressure rises  -  that's how anticyclones (highs) build. If air flows away from an area aloft (divergence), the column gets lighter and surface pressure falls  -  that's how depressions (lows) deepen. It's like stacking or removing weights. This is why weather maps change day to day: air is constantly being redistributed, changing the weight of air columns across NZ and creating the parade of highs and lows that drives our weather.
exam_tip: Air added to the column above = surface pressure rises (high builds). Air removed from the column above = surface pressure falls (low deepens). Think of it as adding or removing weight.
key_facts: Surface pressure = weight of the air column above
Adding air to the column (convergence aloft) increases surface pressure
Removing air from the column (divergence aloft) decreases surface pressure
Anticyclones build when air converges aloft
Depressions deepen when air diverges aloft
Constant redistribution of air creates weather patterns

syllabus_desc: Define the International Standard Atmosphere (ISA).
concept: The International Standard Atmosphere (ISA) is a theoretical model of the atmosphere that provides a common reference for calibrating altimeters and comparing aircraft performance. ISA conditions at sea level are: temperature 15°C, pressure 1013.25 hPa, and a temperature lapse rate of 1.98°C per 1,000 ft (roughly 2°C per 1,000 ft) through the troposphere. These are the conditions your altimeter is calibrated to. Of course, the real atmosphere almost never matches ISA exactly  -  that's why you need to set QNH and why performance calculations include corrections for non-standard conditions.
exam_tip: ISA at sea level: 15°C, 1013.25 hPa. Lapse rate: ~2°C per 1,000 ft. ISA at 5,000 ft = 5°C, ISA at 10,000 ft = -5°C. Your altimeter assumes ISA conditions  -  deviations cause errors.
key_facts: ISA at sea level: 15°C and 1013.25 hPa
Temperature lapse rate: 1.98°C per 1,000 ft (~2°C per 1,000 ft)
Theoretical model used for altimeter calibration and performance data
ISA temperature at 5,000 ft = 5°C
ISA temperature at 10,000 ft = -5°C
Real atmosphere rarely matches ISA exactly

syllabus_desc: Describe how New Zealand conditions differ from ISA.
concept: New Zealand conditions frequently differ from ISA, and that matters for your flying. NZ's average sea level temperature is generally close to ISA in the North Island but often cooler than ISA in the South Island, particularly in winter. The maritime climate means that temperature extremes are moderated compared to continental regions. Pressure systems regularly cause QNH values well above or below 1013 hPa  -  deep lows can bring QNH below 990 hPa, while strong anticyclones can push it above 1030 hPa. NZ's mid-latitude location (34-47°S) means the tropopause is lower than at the equator but higher than at the poles. These differences mean you should always work with actual conditions, not assumed ISA values.
exam_tip: NZ conditions often differ from ISA. South Island winters are typically colder than ISA. QNH varies widely with passing weather systems. Always use actual temperature and QNH for performance calculations, not ISA assumptions.
key_facts: NZ average temperatures roughly match ISA in the North but often cooler in the South
Maritime climate moderates temperature extremes
QNH frequently differs from 1013 hPa due to passing weather systems
Deep lows: QNH below 990 hPa. Strong highs: QNH above 1030 hPa
NZ tropopause typically around 10 km (lower than equatorial, higher than polar)
Always use actual conditions for performance calculations

syllabus_desc: Explain how deviation from ISA values influences performance of aircraft and aircraft engines.
concept: When conditions differ from ISA, your aircraft's performance changes  -  and it's always worse when it's hotter or the pressure is lower than standard. Higher than ISA temperature means lower air density, which means less lift from the wings and less power from the engine. The propeller also becomes less efficient in thinner air. The result: longer takeoff rolls, reduced climb rates, and higher true airspeeds for the same indicated airspeed. A hot day at an inland NZ aerodrome like Taupo or Alexandra can significantly degrade performance. Performance charts in your flight manual use ISA as the baseline and provide corrections for deviations. Always check the actual temperature against ISA before committing to a short strip.
exam_tip: Hotter than ISA = worse performance (lower density = less lift, less power, longer takeoff, reduced climb). Colder than ISA = better performance. Use density altitude to assess actual performance conditions.
key_facts: Higher than ISA temperature = lower air density = reduced performance
Lower pressure than ISA = lower air density = reduced performance
Effects: longer takeoff rolls, reduced climb rate, less engine power
True airspeed increases for the same indicated airspeed in hot/low pressure
Performance charts use ISA as baseline with correction factors
Hot days at inland NZ aerodromes require careful performance calculations

syllabus_desc: Define QNH and altitude.
concept: QNH is the atmospheric pressure at mean sea level (MSL) for a given location. When you set QNH on your altimeter subscale, the altimeter reads your altitude  -  your height above mean sea level. This is the setting you'll use for most of your flying in NZ. It's essential because it gives everyone a common reference: if you and another pilot both set the same QNH, your altimeters will agree on altitude, meaning separation between aircraft is maintained. QNH changes as weather systems move through, which is why ATC gives you regular QNH updates and you need to update your subscale accordingly.
exam_tip: QNH set = altimeter reads ALTITUDE (height above mean sea level). QNH is the most commonly used altimeter setting in NZ. If QNH changes and you don't update, your altimeter will read incorrectly.
key_facts: QNH = atmospheric pressure at mean sea level
Altimeter with QNH set reads altitude (height above MSL)
Most commonly used altimeter setting in NZ
Provides common reference for all aircraft in an area
QNH changes with passing weather systems
Must be updated regularly from ATC or ATIS
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Define QFE and height.
concept: QFE is the atmospheric pressure at the aerodrome reference point (typically the runway threshold). When you set QFE on your altimeter subscale, the altimeter reads your height  -  your vertical distance above that aerodrome. On the ground at the aerodrome, your altimeter will read approximately zero. QFE is useful for circuit flying because it directly tells you your height above the airfield. However, in NZ, QNH is the standard setting for most operations, and QFE is less commonly used than in some other countries. Some NZ flight schools use QFE for circuit training, while others stick with QNH throughout.
exam_tip: QFE set = altimeter reads HEIGHT (above aerodrome level). On the ground at the aerodrome, altimeter reads approximately zero. QNH is more commonly used in NZ than QFE. Know the difference between altitude (MSL reference) and height (ground reference).
key_facts: QFE = atmospheric pressure at the aerodrome reference point
Altimeter with QFE set reads height (above aerodrome level)
On the ground at the aerodrome, altimeter reads approximately zero
Useful for circuit flying (direct height above airfield)
Less commonly used in NZ than QNH
Height = vertical distance above a ground reference point
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25, AIPNZ ENR

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of changes in MSL pressure on aircraft in flight, and why a pressure altimeter requires a subscale adjustment.
concept: Here's the classic trap: if you fly from an area of high pressure to an area of low pressure without updating your altimeter subscale, your altimeter will over-read  -  it'll show you higher than you actually are. The old saying 'high to low, look out below' captures this perfectly. The reverse is also true: flying from low to high pressure, your altimeter under-reads (shows you lower than you are). In NZ, where weather systems move through regularly, QNH can change significantly over a short flight. That's why you need to update your subscale whenever ATC gives you a new QNH, or when transitioning between different QNH areas. Getting this wrong means you might not have the terrain clearance you think you have.
exam_tip: 'High to low, look out below'  -  flying into lower pressure without updating QNH means your altimeter OVER-reads (you're lower than indicated). Always update QNH as you fly. This is a very commonly tested concept.
key_facts: Flying from high to low pressure: altimeter over-reads (higher than actual)
Flying from low to high pressure: altimeter under-reads (lower than actual)
'High to low, look out below'  -  classic aviation mnemonic
Must update QNH when ATC provides new values
NZ weather systems cause frequent QNH changes
Failing to update QNH can result in insufficient terrain clearance

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of correct subscale setting.
concept: Setting the correct altimeter subscale isn't just good practice  -  it's genuinely a safety-of-flight matter. An incorrect subscale means your altimeter is lying to you about your altitude, and in NZ's mountainous terrain that can be fatal. A 1 hPa error in your subscale equates to roughly a 30 ft altitude error. If your QNH is 10 hPa out, you could be 300 ft lower than you think  -  that's significant when you're flying through valleys in the Southern Alps or on approach in marginal conditions. Always cross-check your altimeter against the aerodrome elevation when on the ground, and update QNH religiously throughout your flight.
exam_tip: 1 hPa subscale error ≈ 30 ft altitude error. Always verify your altimeter reads aerodrome elevation (within 50 ft) when on the ground with the correct QNH set. In NZ mountain terrain, subscale errors can be lethal.
key_facts: Correct subscale is a safety-of-flight issue
1 hPa subscale error ≈ 30 ft altitude error
Verify altimeter against known aerodrome elevation on the ground
Altimeter should read within 50 ft of aerodrome elevation with correct QNH
Update QNH throughout the flight from ATC or ATIS
Especially critical in NZ mountainous terrain

syllabus_desc: Define the measurement of the standard surface wind in aviation meteorological reports and forecasts.
concept: The standard surface wind measurement in NZ aviation is taken at a height of 10 metres above ground level at the aerodrome. This represents the wind conditions you'll encounter during takeoff and landing operations. MetService and AIPNZ reports use this 10-metre standard so that all aerodromes - from Ardmore to Queenstown - report consistently. Wind direction is given as the direction FROM which the wind is blowing (not where it's going), and speed is reported as a mean value over a 10-minute observation period.
exam_tip: Remember: surface wind is measured at 10 metres height, and it's always reported as the direction the wind is COMING FROM, not going to.
key_facts: Standard surface wind measurement height: 10 metres above ground level
Wind direction: where the wind is blowing FROM, not towards
Wind speed: mean value calculated over a 10-minute observation period
Used consistently across all NZ aerodromes for standardized reporting
This 10-metre wind differs from upper-air winds measured at higher altitudes
Essential for takeoff and landing performance calculations at your aerodrome
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25; AIPNZ meteorological reporting standards

syllabus_desc: State the units used to describe wind speed.
concept: In NZ aviation, wind speed is reported in knots (kt), which you'll see in METAR reports from Wellington, Christchurch, Hamilton, and other aerodromes. One knot equals one nautical mile per hour, and it's the standard unit across all ICAO-compliant meteorological reporting. MetService reports use knots for consistency with international standards. You might occasionally encounter metres per second in academic texts, but for operational flying in New Zealand, think knots - that's what's on your ATIS and what you'll brief before every flight.
exam_tip: Knots (kt) is the only unit used in operational NZ aviation meteorology. Don't confuse it with mph or m/s.
key_facts: Primary unit: knots (kt) = nautical miles per hour
1 knot = 1.852 kilometres per hour = 0.5144 metres per second
All METAR, TAF, and ATC reports use knots
International ICAO standard for consistency across regions
Metres per second (m/s) and kilometres per hour are NOT used in NZ flight operations
Essential for calculating takeoff and landing distances, fuel planning, and performance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25; ICAO Annex 3

syllabus_desc: State the units used to describe wind direction with reference to (a) forecasts and observations issued by MetService; (b) spot winds relayed to pilots by Air Traffic Control.
concept: Here's a critical distinction in NZ aviation: MetService forecasts and METAR observations report wind direction in DEGREES TRUE (measured from true north), while ATC spot winds relayed to you as a pilot are given in DEGREES MAGNETIC (referenced to magnetic north). This is crucial - when you're planning your flight with the forecast and thinking about your compass heading, you're working with magnetic. But when MetService tells you the wind direction at Ardmore, they've given you true. You'll need to apply the local magnetic variation to convert between the two. At Wellington, for example, variation is about 25°E, while at Queenstown it's closer to 22°E. Always confirm which reference frame you're using to avoid confusion in the cockpit.
exam_tip: Critical NZ distinction: MetService reports use DEGREES TRUE; ATC spot winds use DEGREES MAGNETIC. Know the conversion for your region.
key_facts: MetService forecasts and METAR observations: DEGREES TRUE (referenced to true north)
ATC spot winds to pilots: DEGREES MAGNETIC (referenced to magnetic north)
Magnetic variation across NZ ranges from approximately 20°E to 26°E
Wellington area: ~25°E variation; Queenstown area: ~22°E variation
Always confirm reference frame when receiving wind information
Understanding this distinction prevents dangerous navigation and planning errors
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25; AIPNZ; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: List the three forces acting to generate wind at low-levels.
concept: Wind at low levels - the altitudes where you're flying - is generated by three forces working together. The pressure gradient force is the primary driver: differences in atmospheric pressure between high and low pressure systems push air around the globe. The Coriolis force then deflects that moving air, especially as it travels longer distances. Finally, friction at the surface (from terrain, vegetation, and buildings) slows the wind and reduces the Coriolis deflection. Over rough terrain like the Southern Alps or the hills around Hamilton, friction is much stronger than over open ocean. Together, these three forces determine the wind you'll actually encounter at 1,000 feet over Paraparaumu or while climbing out of Milford Sound.
exam_tip: Three forces: pressure gradient (driver), Coriolis (deflector), and friction (modifier). Remember them in that order of introduction.
key_facts: Pressure gradient force: primary driver, caused by pressure differences
Coriolis force: deflects moving air due to Earth's rotation
Friction: reduces wind speed and weakens Coriolis effect near the surface
These three forces are in balance (geostrophic balance) at upper levels
Friction is strongest over land (especially rough terrain) and weakest over sea
The interplay of these forces creates the variable wind patterns you encounter in the friction layer

syllabus_desc: Outline the cause of Coriolis force.
concept: The Coriolis force isn't a real force - it's a deflection that happens because the Earth is rotating. Imagine you're standing on a rotating platform and throw a ball straight ahead. To someone standing still off the platform, the ball goes straight, but from your perspective on the rotating surface, it curves sideways. That's the Coriolis effect. The Earth rotates from west to east, and as air moves across the surface in the Northern Hemisphere, this rotation makes moving air appear to deflect to the right. In the Southern Hemisphere (that's us in New Zealand), moving air appears to deflect to the left. It's purely an artefact of our rotating reference frame - nothing actually pushes the air sideways, but the mathematics of rotating frames of reference predict this deflection perfectly.
exam_tip: Coriolis is caused by Earth's rotation; it's not a real force, just an apparent deflection in a rotating reference frame.
key_facts: Caused by Earth's rotation from west to east
It's an apparent force in a rotating reference frame, not a real physical force
Deflects moving objects to the RIGHT in Northern Hemisphere
Deflects moving objects to the LEFT in Southern Hemisphere (important for NZ)
Effect increases with latitude: stronger at poles, zero at the equator
Deflects air perpendicular to its direction of motion

syllabus_desc: List the three properties of Coriolis force.
concept: Coriolis force has three defining properties that shape every wind pattern you'll study. First, it acts perpendicular to the wind direction - if wind is moving north, Coriolis deflects it east or west, never head-on. Second, it increases with the speed of the moving air: faster winds experience stronger deflection. Third, it depends on latitude: it's maximum at the poles and zero at the equator. This is why the jet stream at high latitudes deflects more dramatically than light winds near the tropics. In New Zealand, we're at around 40° south latitude, so Coriolis is quite significant - strong enough that it fundamentally shapes our anticyclones and low-pressure systems. This is why high-pressure systems in the Southern Hemisphere rotate clockwise (opposite to the Northern Hemisphere), and understanding these properties helps you predict wind shifts as you climb or fly across different latitudes.
exam_tip: Three properties: acts perpendicular to wind direction, increases with wind speed, and varies with latitude. Memorize this trio.
key_facts: Acts perpendicular to the direction of wind motion
Magnitude increases with wind speed (stronger winds = stronger deflection)
Magnitude increases with latitude (maximum at poles, zero at equator)
Maximum effect at high latitudes like New Zealand (40°S)
Zero effect at the equator, allowing tropical winds to behave differently
These properties explain why pressure systems rotate differently in Southern Hemisphere

syllabus_desc: Define the 'geostrophic wind'.
concept: The geostrophic wind is the theoretical wind that would result if only the pressure gradient force and the Coriolis force were acting - no friction involved. It's a perfectly balanced state where these two forces are equal and opposite, so the wind flows parallel to the isobars on a weather map. You can't measure a true geostrophic wind at ground level because friction is always present, but at around 1,500 feet and above over fairly smooth terrain (or free of obstacles), the wind approaches geostrophic conditions. This concept is powerful because it lets you read a pressure pattern map and estimate what wind direction and speed you should expect aloft. At Paraparaumu or over the ocean near Wellington, the wind at 2,000 feet comes much closer to geostrophic than the surface wind does. Understanding geostrophic wind is your first step to interpreting pressure systems and forecasting wind changes with altitude.
exam_tip: Geostrophic wind: pressure gradient and Coriolis balanced, wind flows parallel to isobars, no friction assumed.
key_facts: Defined when pressure gradient force equals Coriolis force
Wind flows parallel to isobars in this balanced state
Friction is NOT a factor in geostrophic wind
Theoretical condition approached at ~1,500 feet and above
Cannot exist at the surface due to friction layer
Useful for estimating upper-air wind from pressure patterns

syllabus_desc: Explain how friction affects the surface wind velocity.
concept: Friction from terrain, vegetation, buildings, and water surfaces slows the wind down at ground level. This reduction in wind speed weakens the Coriolis force (remember, Coriolis strength depends on wind speed), which means the wind can't maintain its balanced geostrophic state. The result: surface wind velocity is typically 30 to 50 percent lower than the geostrophic wind at the same location, depending on terrain roughness. Over smooth water, friction is minimal and surface wind might be 70 to 80 percent of geostrophic. Over rough, built-up terrain like Auckland or Wellington's suburban sprawl, friction is much stronger and surface wind might be only 40 to 50 percent of geostrophic. This is why ATC can tell you wind at 1,000 feet is stronger and more consistent than what you see at the surface, especially on approaches into Hamilton or Christchurch where the friction layer is thick and rough.
exam_tip: Friction reduces surface wind to 30-50% of geostrophic wind; effect depends on terrain roughness.
key_facts: Friction acts opposite to wind motion, reducing velocity
Weakens Coriolis force by reducing wind speed
Destroys geostrophic balance near the surface
Over smooth water: surface wind ~70-80% of geostrophic
Over rough terrain: surface wind ~30-50% of geostrophic
Wind speed increases dramatically climbing out of the friction layer

syllabus_desc: Explain what is meant by the 'friction layer'.
concept: The friction layer (also called the boundary layer or mixing layer) is the lowest section of the atmosphere where friction from the Earth's surface significantly affects wind. Below this layer, friction dominates and wind is slowed, turbulent, and often variable. Above this layer, friction becomes negligible and wind approaches the geostrophic pattern. In New Zealand, the friction layer typically extends from the ground up to roughly 1,000 to 1,500 feet, though this varies depending on terrain and atmospheric stability. When you're taking off from Ardmore, you're in the friction layer; when you climb through 2,000 feet, you're approaching the free atmosphere. The friction layer is where you'll experience the most turbulence, wind shear, and wind variability. This layer traps warm air and moisture, which is also why thermals and cumulus clouds often develop within it.
exam_tip: Friction layer: typically 1,000-1,500 feet deep in NZ, where surface friction dominates wind behavior.
key_facts: Layer where surface friction significantly affects wind
Typical depth: 1,000 to 1,500 feet in New Zealand
Depth varies with terrain roughness and atmospheric stability
Characterized by wind shear, turbulence, and variability
Wind increases with altitude through the friction layer
Above friction layer, wind approaches geostrophic conditions

syllabus_desc: Describe the elements that influence the depth of the 'friction layer'.
concept: The friction layer isn't a fixed boundary - its depth changes depending on how rough the terrain is and how stable the atmosphere is. Over open ocean, the friction layer might be only 500 feet deep because there's little to disturb the air. Over rugged, built-up terrain like the Manawatu or around Auckland, it can extend to 2,000 feet or deeper because buildings, trees, and hills create constant turbulence. The second major factor is atmospheric stability. On a cold morning when the atmosphere is stable (temperature increases with height), vertical mixing is suppressed and the friction layer stays shallow. On a hot afternoon when the atmosphere is unstable (warm air rises freely), the friction layer deepens significantly as thermal convection reaches higher. Time of day matters too: the friction layer is deeper in afternoon heat and shallower in the stable early morning. Understanding these variations helps you predict wind shear and turbulence on approach into different aerodromes.
exam_tip: Friction layer depth depends on terrain roughness (deeper over land) and atmospheric stability (deeper when unstable).
key_facts: Terrain roughness: rougher terrain = deeper friction layer
Over smooth water: friction layer ~500-800 feet
Over rough/built-up terrain: friction layer ~1,500-2,000 feet
Atmospheric stability: stable air = shallow layer, unstable air = deep layer
Diurnal cycle: layer deeper in afternoon, shallower in early morning
Depth affects wind shear intensity and turbulence characteristics

syllabus_desc: Define the following terms: (a) gust; (b) squall; (c) veering; (d) backing.
concept: These four terms describe wind changes you'll encounter on approach or in flight. A gust is a brief, sudden increase in wind speed lasting seconds to minutes - you'll feel it as a bump in the cockpit. A squall is a more sustained and dramatic increase in wind associated with weather activity like a thunderstorm or frontal passage; it can last 10 minutes or more and be dangerous. Veering is when the wind direction shifts clockwise (e.g., from south to southwest to west) - in the Southern Hemisphere, this often means a warm front is approaching or a high-pressure system is moving in. Backing is the opposite: a counter-clockwise shift (e.g., from south to southeast to east), often signalling a low or a cold front. At Ardmore on a northeast wind, if the wind backs to north, you'd know the pressure pattern is shifting. Watch for wind changes during descent into Wellington or Christchurch - they're often your first warning of significant weather movement.
exam_tip: Gust = sudden brief speed increase; squall = sustained dramatic increase. Veering = clockwise shift; backing = counter-clockwise.
key_facts: Gust: brief, sudden increase in wind speed (seconds to minutes)
Squall: sustained, dramatic wind increase associated with weather systems
Veering: clockwise wind direction shift (S→SW→W); indicates warming/high pressure
Backing: counter-clockwise wind direction shift (S→SE→E); indicates cooling/low pressure
In Southern Hemisphere, veering ahead of a system = warm front; backing = cold front
Changes in these patterns are forecasting tools and safety indicators

syllabus_desc: Describe the diurnal variation of the surface wind over: (a) land; (b) sea.
concept: Wind behaves very differently over land versus sea due to how they heat and cool. Over land, the surface heats rapidly during the day, creating thermal turbulence and increasing wind speeds - you'll find the strongest surface winds in the afternoon, especially around 14:00-16:00 local time. As the sun sets, the land cools quickly and the wind weakens, often dropping to a few knots by sunrise. Over the sea, heating is more gradual and cooling less dramatic, so the diurnal variation is much smaller. However, sea breezes do develop during the day - warm air over land rises, and cooler air from the sea flows in to replace it. This creates a predictable cycle: weak or variable winds in the early morning, sea breeze developing by mid-morning, stronger winds by afternoon, and then the sea breeze weakening or reversing after sunset. At Paraparaumu or Ardmore, you'll see this pattern clearly - early morning flights have calm conditions, but by afternoon, plan for stronger winds and rougher conditions on descent.
exam_tip: Land: strongest winds mid-afternoon, calm at dawn. Sea: smaller variation, but sea breeze circulation develops during day.
key_facts: Over land: surface heats rapidly, wind increases through the day, peaks 14:00-16:00
Over land: rapid cooling at sunset, wind drops significantly by dawn
Over sea: slower heating and cooling, diurnal variation much smaller
Sea breeze circulation: develops morning, strengthens afternoon, weakens/reverses evening
Combined effect: offshore areas like Wellington see sea breeze strength vary diurnally
Critical for flight planning: plan for stronger winds and rougher conditions in afternoon

syllabus_desc: Describe the changes in wind velocity when climbing out of, or descending through, the friction layer.
concept: Wind speed increases significantly as you climb out of the friction layer. At ground level departing Hamilton, the wind might be 10 knots, but by 1,500 feet it could be 20 knots, and by 3,000 feet it's closer to the geostrophic wind and much stronger. This increase is gradual but steady as friction's effect diminishes. The wind direction also becomes more aligned with isobars (more consistent) the higher you climb. Descending is the reverse: as you approach your destination (say, Christchurch), wind speed decreases as you descend through the friction layer, and you'll notice increasing turbulence and variability. The descent is typically rougher than the climb because you're transitioning from smooth, high-altitude air into increasingly turbulent, friction-dominated air. Watch this during your descent: expect wind speeds to drop 30-40 percent by the time you reach pattern altitude, and brief yourself for wind shear, gusts, and direction changes. The transition through the friction layer is where most wind-related surprises happen.
exam_tip: Climbing: wind speed increases, becomes more consistent; descending: wind speed decreases, turbulence increases.
key_facts: Climbing: wind speed increases gradually as friction decreases
Typical increase: 20-40% from surface to 2,000 feet
Wind direction becomes more consistent (more aligned with isobars) with altitude
Descending: wind speed decreases, turbulence increases
Expect wind shear and gusts during descent through friction layer
Rate of change depends on terrain roughness and stability

syllabus_desc: Describe the limitations of windsocks in New Zealand.
concept: Windsocks at aerodromes like Ardmore, Paraparaumu, and Milford Sound are useful but have real limitations you need to know about. First, a windsock shows local wind direction and relative strength, but it's affected by nearby buildings, hangars, and terrain - the wind it shows might not represent the wind 500 feet above the runway. Second, in light wind conditions (under about 5 knots), the windsock hangs limply and doesn't clearly indicate direction; use ATC wind information instead. Third, windsocks give you a snapshot at one location; they don't show wind shear or variation across the airfield. Finally, terrain channelling around NZ aerodromes (especially Wellington, Queenstown, and Cook Strait areas) can create dramatic wind variations that a single windsock won't capture. Always cross-check the windsock against the ATIS wind and any ATC information. Don't rely solely on a windsock for landing decisions - it's one tool among many in your wind assessment.
exam_tip: Windsocks are affected by local terrain, unreliable in light wind, and show only one point on the airfield.
key_facts: Affected by nearby buildings, hangars, and terrain obstructions
Unreliable indicator in winds under ~5 knots (hangs limply)
Shows only local conditions at its specific location
Does not represent wind at altitude above the runway
Cannot show wind shear variation across the airfield
Should always be cross-checked with ATIS and ATC information
source: NZ CAA AC 61-3 Rev 25; CAR Part 91

syllabus_desc: Describe how an approximate wind direction can be determined from: (a) ripples on water; (b) wind lanes on water; (c) wind shadow on bodies of water; (d) cloud shadows.
concept: Out over the Tasman or approaching a coastal aerodrome, you can read the wind from surface and cloud clues if your instruments fail or as a backup. Ripples on water form perpendicular to the wind direction - if ripples run north-south, the wind is east-west. Wind lanes (long, parallel streaks of foam or disturbance) also run perpendicular to the wind, giving you a clear pattern. Wind shadow is the smoother area downwind of an island or headland where the wind is blocked - the wind direction is toward the shadow, not away from it. Cloud shadows work similarly: they form on the downwind side of terrain or islands, showing you wind direction. These techniques are survival knowledge - if your wind direction indicator fails during a flight from Ardmore to Great Barrier Island, you can use the sea surface pattern to estimate wind direction and confirm your heading. Practice reading these clues during your training flights so you're prepared if you ever need them.
exam_tip: Ripples and wind lanes = perpendicular to wind. Shadows = downwind side. Wind = toward shadow.
key_facts: Water ripples: form perpendicular to wind direction
Wind lanes: parallel foam streaks perpendicular to wind direction
Wind shadow: smoother water on downwind side of obstruction; wind blows toward shadow
Cloud shadow: forms downwind of terrain; shows wind direction is toward shadow
Useful as backup if instruments fail or as visual confirmation
Practice during training to develop recognition skill

syllabus_desc: State Buys Ballot's Law.
concept: Buys Ballot's Law is a simple rule that links wind direction to pressure patterns. Stand with the wind at your back, and the low-pressure area is roughly on your left, and high pressure is on your right. Simple enough - until you remember we're in the Southern Hemisphere. In the Northern Hemisphere, low pressure is to your RIGHT when you face into the wind. In New Zealand, it's reversed: face into the wind, and low pressure is to your LEFT, high pressure to your RIGHT. This difference is fundamental to understanding why our cyclones rotate clockwise and Northern Hemisphere ones rotate counter-clockwise. At Wellington, if you face into a northerly wind, you know there's relatively lower pressure to your left (west) and higher pressure to your right (east). This law works because the wind is deflected by Coriolis: it blows roughly along isobars with low pressure on the deflected (left, for us) side.
exam_tip: Southern Hemisphere Buys Ballot: face into wind, low pressure LEFT, high pressure RIGHT. Opposite of Northern Hemisphere.
key_facts: Stand with wind at back: low pressure on left, high pressure on right (Southern Hemisphere)
Equivalently: face into wind, low pressure on left, high pressure on right
Opposite in Northern Hemisphere: low pressure on RIGHT when facing into wind
Based on Coriolis deflection of wind along isobars
Works for any scale of pressure pattern from anticyclones to fronts
Essential for identifying pressure systems and predicting wind shifts

syllabus_desc: Explain how applying Buys Ballot's Law can: (a) determine the location of high and low pressure areas; (b) be used as a basic forecasting tool.
concept: Buys Ballot's Law is more than just a memory aid - it's a practical tool for understanding pressure patterns and forecasting wind shifts. First, it lets you locate pressure systems from the wind you're observing. If the wind at Ardmore is northeast and you stand with it at your back, you know pressure is dropping (low) toward the northwest and building (high) toward the southeast. This tells you which direction a front or low might be moving. Second, it's a forecasting tool: if you notice the wind backing (shifting counter-clockwise from south to southeast to east), you know by Buys Ballot that the low-pressure area is moving to your left - meaning the front and worse weather are approaching from the west. If the wind is veering (shifting clockwise), the high is moving in and weather improves. Many experienced pilots use this simple rule to interpret wind changes during a flight and predict what's coming. Over the Tasman or during a flight from Hamilton to Wellington, watching wind shifts and applying Buys Ballot helps you anticipate frontal passages and plan your flight accordingly.
exam_tip: Use Buys Ballot to locate pressure systems from wind direction, and predict wind/weather shifts from wind direction changes.
key_facts: Wind direction directly indicates nearby pressure system locations (low to left, high to right when facing into wind)
Backing wind (counter-clockwise shift) = low pressure moving left, worse weather approaching
Veering wind (clockwise shift) = high pressure moving in, weather improving
Can predict frontal approach from changes in wind pattern
Works at any scale: local sea breeze circulation or synoptic pressure systems
Practical forecasting tool when ground-based observations are your only data

syllabus_desc: Describe the development of sea breezes with reference to: (a) horizontal and vertical limits around New Zealand; (b) timing of the occurrence; (c) average strength of the sea breeze; (d) associated cloud and precipitation; (e) associated turbulence.
concept: Sea breezes are one of the most predictable and important local wind features you'll encounter in New Zealand aviation. They develop because land heats more rapidly than adjacent sea, creating a pressure difference that drives cool air inland. (a) Horizontal limits: a sea breeze typically penetrates inland 10-40 km depending on terrain - at Wellington, it can extend inland across the Tasman; around Auckland, it reaches into the suburbs. Vertical limits: it usually extends 1,500-2,500 feet above ground, sometimes higher over steep terrain. (b) Timing: sea breezes typically develop by mid-morning (10:00-11:00), peak mid-afternoon (14:00-16:00), and decay after sunset. They're strongest in high-pressure systems with light upper-level wind. (c) Strength: typical sea breeze is 8-15 knots, occasionally stronger in Cook Strait or around Paraparaumu where channelling enhances it. (d) Cloud: cumulus often develops along the sea-breeze front and inland, with occasional light showers. (e) Turbulence: moderate turbulence at the sea-breeze front and along its boundaries; smoother within the breeze itself. Understanding sea-breeze timing helps you plan approaches - land approaches at Ardmore before the sea breeze peaks, or factor in stronger winds if arriving later.
exam_tip: Sea breeze: develops mid-morning, peaks mid-afternoon, 10-40 km inland penetration, 8-15 knots typical, turbulent at front.
key_facts: Develops due to differential heating of land vs. sea
Horizontal penetration inland: 10-40 km depending on terrain
Vertical extent: typically 1,500-2,500 feet, sometimes higher over mountains
Typical timing: develops 10:00-11:00, peaks 14:00-16:00, decays after sunset
Typical strength: 8-15 knots, stronger in narrow channels (Cook Strait)
Associated cumulus cloud along sea-breeze front
Moderate turbulence at frontal boundary; smoother within sea breeze

syllabus_desc: Describe the development of katabatic winds with reference to: (a) timing of the occurrence; (b) average strength of katabatic winds over New Zealand.
concept: Katabatic winds are cold, gravity-driven winds that flow downslope - you'll experience them descending from mountain valleys or high terrain toward lower elevations. They develop because cold air over elevated terrain becomes denser and heavier, and simply flows downhill under gravity. In New Zealand, katabatic winds are most pronounced in the Southern Alps around Milford Sound and Queenstown, and in areas like the Manawatu Gap. (a) Timing: katabatic winds strengthen during the evening and night as the high terrain loses heat and becomes colder, then weaken during the day as the sun warms the slopes. They're strongest in the hour or two after sunset and continue into the early morning. (b) Strength: over NZ mountains, katabatic winds are typically 10-20 knots, but can exceed 30 knots in narrow valleys like Milford Sound or the Arthur's Pass area.
