Strong katabatic winds (>8 knots) suppress radiation fog entirely by vigorous mechanical mixing, replacing cold valley air with warmer air from the slope. In NZ mountain valleys, katabatic winds can clear morning fog within an hour if they strengthen, or they can maintain it if they remain weak. Pilots planning flights in alpine valleys must assess katabatic wind strength: weak winds allow fog to persist, strong winds clear it - but in the transition, visibility can be variable and dangerous.
exam_tip: Weak katabatic = mixes gently, fog persists. Moderate katabatic = breaks up fog. Strong katabatic = clears fog. Mountain valleys: watch for wind shift to signal fog clearing.
key_facts: Weak katabatic (<3 kt): light stirring, can enhance fog persistence
Moderate katabatic (3-8 kt): mixing warms and breaks up fog layer
Strong katabatic (>8 kt): vigorous mixing, fog suppressed or cleared
Katabatic wind speed depends on slope steepness and temperature difference
Alpine valleys susceptible: Central Otago, Fiordland, Queenstown basin
Fog can clear rapidly with strengthening katabatic wind (pilot hazard)

syllabus_desc: Describe the operational problems associated with fog.
concept: Fog creates multiple operational hazards for aircraft. First, visibility is severely reduced (often <1 km), limiting ability to see runway, terrain, and other traffic. Second, fog can be sudden: a clear departure becomes an IFR arrival with no time for reroute. Third, fog is often shallow (200-500 ft thick), so it sits at or just above runway level, making approaches impossible while surrounding terrain is clear - diversion is difficult. Fourth, fog obscures runway visual range (RVR), forcing reliance on instrument approaches (ILS, etc.) which many NZ airfields don't have (Ardmore, Paraparaumu, Milford Sound). Fifth, surface wind can increase with altitude, causing wind shear on approach through foggy layer, increasing landing distance. Sixth, in multi-layered fog or with virga, pilots misjudge altitude and can descend below safe descent altitude. Seventh, go-around becomes risky - once committed to landing in fog, climbing away into a thick layer risks spatial disorientation. Eighth, damp runways in fog reduce braking performance and increase aquaplaning risk. For NZ operations, radiation and advection fog at major airfields (Wellington, Auckland, Christchurch) can shut down operations for hours.
exam_tip: Fog hazards: low visibility, sudden onset, shallow layer above terrain, no RVR capability at small airfields, shear, spatial disorientation, difficult go-around, wet runways. Plan for it - weather diversions exist for a reason.
key_facts: Visibility <1 km, sometimes only 100-200 m in dense fog
Fog can form or thicken rapidly, trapping aircraft
Shallow fog (200-500 ft) sits at runway level while terrain clear
Most NZ airfields lack ILS; fog forces holding or diversion
Wind shear on approach through fog layer increases landing distance
Damp runways reduce braking; aquaplaning risk in heavy precipitation
Go-around in fog risks spatial disorientation and loss of control
Advance planning and diversions essential for fog-prone regions

syllabus_desc: List the hazards of airframe icing to aircraft in flight.
concept: Airframe icing is one of the most serious weather hazards you'll face as a pilot. When supercooled water droplets freeze on your aircraft's surfaces, they accumulate ice that can kill your flight. The dangers are real and varied. Weight increases dramatically  -  even a thin layer of ice adds significant mass. Aerodynamic degradation happens because ice roughens the wing surface and disrupts the airflow, killing lift. Your stall speed goes up, your rate of descent increases, and control effectiveness gets worse. Engine power can drop because ice can block air intakes or damage the engine if ingested. And here's the thing: ice doesn't accumulate evenly. It forms faster on some parts of the airframe, throwing off your centre of gravity and control forces. In severe icing, the ice can build so fast and so much that you can't outclimb it, and you're forced down. In a light aircraft with no de-icing equipment, airframe icing isn't just a problem  -  it's potentially catastrophic. The best strategy is avoidance: don't fly through cloud in icing conditions.
exam_tip: Know the major hazards: weight increase, aerodynamic degradation, stall speed increase, loss of power, uneven accumulation affecting CG and control forces, and the risk of being unable to climb out of icing.
key_facts: Airframe ice accumulation increases aircraft weight significantly
Ice roughens wing surfaces and degrades aerodynamic lift
Stall speed increases; rate of descent increases
Engine power can be lost if air intakes are blocked
Ice distribution affects centre of gravity and control forces
Light aircraft without de-icing can be forced down by ice
Uneven ice accumulation reduces control effectiveness

syllabus_desc: Explain the processes involved in the formation of hoar frost on an aircraft on the ground and in flight.
concept: Hoar frost is ice that forms by direct condensation of water vapour on to aircraft surfaces  -  no liquid water stage involved. On the ground, when your aircraft sits out overnight on a clear, still night with high humidity, atmospheric water vapour condenses directly into solid ice crystals on surfaces colder than the air. It looks like a thin, feathery white coating, and it can form anywhere: wings, fuselage, windscreen, the lot. In flight, hoar frost forms when your aircraft passes through very cold, moist air (usually high altitude) and the fuselage surfaces, which are colder than the surrounding air, collect ice directly from the water vapour. The key difference from rime ice is that hoar frost forms in clear air without visible cloud  -  it's a high-altitude phenomenon. The process is called deposition: water vapour turns straight into ice without becoming liquid. This is why you need to check for hoar frost after a clear, humid night and remove it before flight.
exam_tip: Hoar frost forms by direct condensation of water vapour (deposition)  -  no liquid stage. On the ground: clear nights, high humidity, surfaces colder than air. In flight: high altitude, cold, moist air, fuselage colder than surrounding air. It looks feathery and white.
key_facts: Hoar frost forms by deposition (water vapour → ice directly)
No liquid water stage involved
On ground: clear, still nights with high humidity
In flight: high altitude, cold, moist air masses
Fuselage surfaces are colder than surrounding air
Appears as fine, feathery white coating
Can accumulate on any exposed aircraft surface
Can form in clear air (no visible cloud)

syllabus_desc: State the dangers of hoar frost and the actions required to alleviate these dangers on the ground and in flight.
concept: Hoar frost might look innocent, but it's a serious hazard. A thin layer of hoar frost disrupts airflow over your wings and control surfaces, causing significant loss of lift and increase in drag. This means higher stall speed, reduced climb performance, and longer take-off roll. In a light aircraft, even a light coating can degrade performance enough to prevent take-off or climb. On the ground, your pre-flight checks must include visual inspection for hoar frost on all exposed surfaces  -  wings, fuselage, tail, windscreen, and control surfaces. If you find it, you must remove it completely before flight. You can do this by gently brushing it off (never use abrasive materials), or if it's stubborn, by using warm water or approved de-icing fluid. Get it all off  -  even a light coating matters. In flight, hoar frost is harder to deal with. You can't de-ice it with standard hot-air systems because those are designed for rime ice accumulation in cloud, not hoar frost at altitude. The best strategy is avoidance: plan your flight to avoid high-altitude cloud in humid conditions where hoar frost forms. If you encounter it, you may need to descend to warmer air to stop accumulation.
exam_tip: On ground: visually inspect all surfaces, remove completely before flight (brush gently, use warm water or approved de-icing fluid). In flight: avoid the conditions that cause it (high-altitude cloud in humid air). Hoar frost reduces lift and increases drag.
key_facts: Hoar frost disrupts airflow and reduces lift significantly
Increases drag and stall speed
Reduces climb performance and increases take-off roll
On ground: pre-flight visual inspection essential
Remove by gentle brushing, warm water, or approved fluid
Never use abrasive materials
Remove completely before flight
In flight: descend to warmer air to stop accumulation
Standard de-ice systems are not effective against hoar frost

syllabus_desc: Explain why flight in cloud above the freezing-level can be very hazardous.
concept: Flying in cloud above the freezing level is hazardous because that's where airframe icing happens. At the freezing level (0°C), the air temperature is at the threshold where supercooled water droplets exist  -  these are liquid water droplets at temperatures below 0°C that don't freeze until they hit your aircraft. The problem intensifies as you climb above the freezing level because the air becomes progressively colder, and supercooled droplets become more abundant, especially in thick cumulus or cumulonimbus cloud. When these droplets strike your airframe, they freeze instantly on contact, building ice very quickly. Rime ice forms in this environment, and it can accumulate at frightening rates in areas of high liquid water content. Your engine can ingest supercooled droplets and lose power. Carburetor ice becomes a secondary risk. The wing lift degrades, drag increases, stall speed goes up, and if you can't climb out or descend out of the cloud, you're in trouble. In NZ, the freezing level varies seasonally and geographically, but it's typically around 3,000-4,500 feet in winter and higher in summer. If you're flying VFR in a light aircraft and you hit cloud above the freezing level, you're in serious danger because you have no ice-protection equipment. The answer is simple: don't go there. Check the freezing level from the ARFORs and TAFs, stay below it in VFR conditions, or avoid cloud entirely.
exam_tip: Above the freezing level (0°C), supercooled water droplets exist and freeze on contact with your aircraft. Rime ice forms rapidly. Engine power can be lost. Lift is degraded, drag increases, stall speed increases. Light aircraft have no protection. Avoidance is essential.
key_facts: Above freezing level, supercooled water droplets exist
Supercooled droplets freeze instantly on aircraft contact
Rime ice accumulates rapidly in clouds above freezing level
Engine can ingest supercooled droplets and lose power
Wing lift degrades; drag increases; stall speed increases
NZ freezing level typically 3,000-4,500 ft in winter, higher in summer
Light aircraft have no ice-protection equipment
Best strategy: stay below freezing level in VFR flight
Check freezing level in ARFORs and TAFs before flight

syllabus_desc: Explain how to avoid or alleviate all forms of airframe icing other than hoar frost.
concept: For rime ice, clear ice, and mixed ice accumulation in flight, you have several options  -  though honestly, the best one is avoidance. First, know the conditions: rime ice forms in cloud with small droplets (typical of stratus clouds); clear ice forms with larger droplets (cumulonimbus, freezing rain conditions); mixed ice is a combination. If you're flying a light aircraft without de-icing equipment, your best strategy is to avoid flying through cloud above the freezing level altogether. Check your ARFORs and TAFs, check the freezing level, and plan your route to stay below it in visual conditions. If you do encounter icing in flight, you have a few options. First, try to climb above the cloud if there's a cloud-free layer above  -  though this only works if the cloud is relatively thin and you can climb fast enough. Second, descend below the cloud to warmer air where the icing process stops. This is often your best bet because the freezing level exists below you, so you'll reach warmer air quickly. Third, if you can, change direction to get out of the icing layer  -  sometimes the icing is localized. Keep engine heat on if you have it to help prevent carburettor icing. Don't try to push through cloud expecting the icing to stop  -  it won't, and you'll lose height and performance. If you're in a modern aircraft with anti-ice or de-ice systems, follow the manufacturer's procedures for activating them, but remember that even de-icing systems have limits and can be overwhelmed in severe icing.
exam_tip: Best strategy: avoidance  -  stay below the freezing level in VFR flight. If you encounter icing: try climbing out, descending to warmer air (usually fastest option), or changing course. Activate de-ice/anti-ice systems if available. Don't try to push through.
key_facts: Avoidance is the primary strategy for light aircraft
Stay below freezing level in VFR flight
Check ARFORs, TAFs, and freezing level before flight
If icing encountered: try climbing out of cloud
Descending to warmer air is usually the best in-flight option
Change direction if icing is localized
Activate engine heat to prevent carburettor icing
Modern aircraft: follow de-ice/anti-ice system procedures
De-ice systems have limits and can be overwhelmed
Never try to push through icing; maintain height priority

syllabus_desc: State the hazards for light aircraft from snow.
concept: Snow presents several hazards to light aircraft, especially on the ground. When snow falls from cloud and accumulates on your airframe, it increases weight and degrades aerodynamics just like ice does  -  but snow can accumulate even faster because the crystals are lighter and stick readily. On the ground, snow accumulation on wings and tail surfaces reduces lift capability and increases drag, requiring longer take-off distance and reducing climb performance. If snow is wet and slightly above 0°C, it adheres strongly and is harder to remove than hoar frost. In flight, snow entering your engine air intakes can block them or cause power loss. Snow in the windscreen reduces visibility. And here's a critical point: snow falling from cloud above the freezing level can transition to freezing rain or sleet if it passes through a warm layer before reaching your aircraft, creating a mixed icing hazard. At aerodromes in winter, check the aerodrome conditions carefully. If snow is on the airfield, your take-off roll will be longer on contaminated runways, and braking action is reduced. Before flight from a snow-covered aerodrome, you must remove all snow from your aircraft completely  -  wings, tail, fuselage, windscreen, and engine air intakes. This is not optional.
exam_tip: Snow hazards: increases weight and drag on ground; can block engine air intakes in flight; reduces visibility if in windscreen. Can transition to freezing rain in flight (icing hazard). Must remove completely before flight. Take-off distance is longer on snow-contaminated runways.
key_facts: Snow accumulation increases weight significantly
Degrades aerodynamic lift; increases drag
Requires longer take-off distance and reduces climb
Snow can enter engine air intakes and cause power loss
Wet snow adheres strongly and is hard to remove
Can transition to freezing rain in flight (icing hazard)
Reduces windscreen visibility
Must be removed completely before flight
Braking action reduced on snow-contaminated runways
Take-off performance degraded on contaminated aerodromes

syllabus_desc: State the hazards for light aircraft from sleet.
concept: Sleet is a nasty hybrid weather phenomenon  -  it's a mix of snow and rain, or rain falling on surfaces below 0°C. When sleet encounters your aircraft, it can form a mixed-icing layer that combines the worst of both worlds. Sleet can accumulate on airframes and freeze solid, building ice very quickly. It's heavier than snow, so even a modest accumulation adds weight rapidly. The ice layer it forms is often harder and more rigid than rime ice, making it mechanically stronger and more likely to stay attached even at higher airspeeds. In flight, sleet from a cloud above the freezing level is a serious icing hazard  -  you'll see the rain droplets and think you're in light precipitation, but as they transition to sleet, they freeze on contact, and icing accelerates. Engine induction and pitot tube icing are significant risks. On the ground, sleet accumulation is messy and difficult to remove because it bonds firmly. If you encounter sleet during your flight, it's a signal that you're in or near freezing rain/icing conditions and you should exit that environment immediately  -  descend or climb or change course to get out of it. Sleet in forecasts (TAFs, ARFORs) is a red flag for icing conditions, and light aircraft should avoid the area entirely.
exam_tip: Sleet is a serious icing hazard  -  mix of snow and rain that freezes on contact. Accumulates quickly and forms hard, dense ice. Heavier than snow, harder to remove. Engine and pitot tube icing risk. Signal of freezing conditions. Avoid.
key_facts: Sleet is rain/snow mix or rain falling on surfaces below 0°C
Forms hard, dense ice layer on aircraft
Accumulates rapidly and adds significant weight
Ice layer is mechanically stronger than rime ice
Engine induction icing is a risk
Pitot tube icing is a significant risk
Difficult to remove on the ground
Signal of freezing rain/icing conditions
In flight: exit immediately if encountered
Light aircraft should avoid areas with sleet in forecasts

syllabus_desc: State the hazards for light aircraft from hail.
concept: Hail is the most violent form of frozen precipitation, and it's almost exclusively found in or near thunderstorms. Hailstones are hard, dense pellets of ice that can range from pea-sized to golf-ball-sized or larger. When hail encounters your aircraft at altitude, it causes mechanical damage  -  it pits and cracks windscreens, dents fuselages, and can damage engine components if ingested. Unlike the gradual accumulation of rime ice, hail impacts are sudden and forceful. Each hailstone that hits your aircraft is a hard impact, not a gentle accretion. This means airframe damage is the primary hazard rather than aerodynamic ice buildup. Engine damage from hail ingestion can be catastrophic. The windscreen damage is particularly dangerous because it reduces visibility and compromises the structural integrity of the cockpit. Hail is found in severe convective clouds, particularly cumulonimbus. You won't find hail in stratus or stratocumulus clouds  -  it's a thunderstorm phenomenon. The solution is simple and absolute: never fly near or through thunderstorms. If you see a thunderstorm on your route, divert around it or cancel the flight. If you encounter hail in flight, it's because you've encountered a severe thunderstorm, and you need to exit that airspace immediately and declare an emergency if necessary.
exam_tip: Hail is hard, dense, found in thunderstorms. Causes mechanical damage  -  pits windscreens, dents fuselage, damages engine components if ingested. Not a gradual accumulation but sudden impacts. Never fly through thunderstorms.

Hailstones range from pea-sized to golf-ball-sized or larger
Causes mechanical impact damage, not gradual accumulation
Pits and cracks windscreens
Dents fuselage and structures
Damages engines if ingested; can be catastrophic
Reduces visibility and compromises cockpit integrity
Found in cumulonimbus clouds (thunderstorms only)
Never fly near or through thunderstorms
If encountered: exit immediately and declare emergency if needed

syllabus_desc: Explain the environmental factors involved in carburettor icing, including: (a) moisture content; (b) temperature; (c) temperature gradient (inversions).
concept: Carburettor icing is a sneaky hazard that forms on the ground or in flight without any visible cloud. It happens inside your carburettor and induction system where you can't see it. Here's the process: as air flows through the carburettor venturi (the narrow section), the air expands and cools dramatically  -  this pressure drop causes rapid cooling, sometimes by 5-10°C or more. If that cooling pushes the air temperature below its dew point, water vapour condenses and freezes directly onto the carburettor walls and throttle valve. Three environmental factors control whether this happens. First, moisture content: if the air is dry, no water vapour condenses, and no ice forms. If the air is humid (high moisture content), there's plenty of water vapour to condense and freeze. That's why carb icing is worst after rain or near the coast where moisture is high. Second, temperature: the actual air temperature matters. If it's too warm, even with moisture, ice won't form. If it's very cold, you might think ice would form readily, but in very cold, dry air (like high altitude in winter), there's not enough moisture for ice to form easily. Carb icing is most dangerous in the temperature range roughly -10°C to +30°C, and worst around 0-20°C where conditions are 'just right' for icing. Third, temperature gradient (inversions): sometimes there's a warm layer aloft with cooler air below. If you climb through an inversion from cold air into a warm layer, the temperature gradient changes, and you might exit carb icing conditions. Conversely, if you climb into colder, moister air aloft, carb icing risk might increase. In NZ, post-frontal conditions often bring moisture-laden air, and the interplay of temperature and moisture is critical to predicting carb icing risk.
exam_tip: Carb icing depends on three factors: (1) Moisture content (dry air = no icing, humid = worst risk); (2) Temperature (most dangerous -10 to +30°C, worst at 0-20°C); (3) Temperature gradient (inversions affect risk). High moisture + moderate temperature = highest risk. Can occur on warm days with high humidity.
key_facts: Carburettor icing forms in the carburettor venturi where air cools
Rapid cooling by pressure drop (5-10°C or more)
Water vapour condenses and freezes on carb walls and throttle
Factor 1  -  Moisture content: dry air = no icing; humid = high risk
Factor 2  -  Temperature: most dangerous -10 to +30°C, worst 0-20°C
Factor 3  -  Temperature gradient (inversions): affect icing risk
Can occur on warm days with high humidity
Worst after rain or near coastal areas (high moisture)
Inversions and temperature layering affect risk
Can't see carb icing forming  -  it's inside the carburettor

syllabus_desc: State the temperature range that carburettor ice typically forms in.
concept: Carburettor ice typically forms in the temperature range approximately -10°C to +30°C, with the highest risk in the range 0-20°C. This is the 'sweet spot' where conditions are just right for icing: the air is cold enough for water droplets to freeze, but warm enough that there's significant moisture available. At very cold temperatures below -10°C, the air is usually very dry, and even though it's cold, there's not enough water vapour to condense and freeze on the carburettor. Above about +30°C, the air is too warm, and even if water vapour condenses, it won't freeze. But in the range 0-20°C on a humid day after rain or near the coast, carburettor icing can form rapidly and without warning. This is why carb icing is so dangerous: it can happen on what looks like a pleasant, warm day  -  you might be flying in clear skies with no visible moisture, yet inside your carburettor, ice is building up and restricting the fuel-air mixture. You won't see it until engine roughness and power loss signal its presence. This is also why it's called 'invisible icing'  -  it forms where you can't see it, under temperature conditions that seem benign.
exam_tip: Carburettor ice typically forms between -10°C and +30°C, with the highest risk in the 0-20°C range. Can occur on warm, humid days in clear air. Temperature inversion or descent into a warm layer can also trigger icing if you then descend into cold, moist air.
key_facts: Typical carb icing temperature range: approximately -10°C to +30°C
Highest risk: 0-20°C (the 'sweet spot')
Below -10°C: air too dry (insufficient moisture)
Above +30°C: air too warm (ice won't form)
Most dangerous in the 0-20°C range
Can occur on warm, sunny days with high humidity
Can occur in clear air with no visible moisture
Engine roughness and power loss signal icing
Invisible  -  you can't see it forming
Particularly dangerous because conditions seem benign

syllabus_desc: Explain how the accretion rate of carburettor ice is influenced by the throttle setting.
concept: Here's a critical point about carburettor icing: the throttle setting has a dramatic effect on how fast ice accumulates. When you reduce throttle (close the throttle valve), the venturi pressure drop becomes more pronounced because the air has to accelerate more to get through the narrower effective throat. This means the temperature drop through the carburettor is greater at reduced throttle than at full throttle. At low throttle settings, the cooling effect is severe, and if conditions are right for carb icing, ice will form faster at reduced throttle than at full throttle. This seems counterintuitive at first  -  you'd think a smaller engine would ice up faster  -  but what matters is the pressure drop and cooling rate through the carburettor. At full power (wide open throttle), the cooling effect is less pronounced because the air isn't forced through as tight a constriction. This is why carburettor icing is such a danger during descent, cruise, or any time you're operating at reduced power. At full throttle, you're less vulnerable to carb icing. Conversely, if you're flying at low power (say, a descent at reduced throttle), your carb icing risk increases significantly. For piston aircraft with carburetor heat systems, applying carb heat during reduced-power flight is essential. You need to warm the incoming air before it enters the carburettor, which raises the dew point and prevents ice formation. This is why the rule of thumb is: if you're flying in conditions where carb icing is possible (humid air, moderate temperature), use carb heat during descent and cruise, and especially at reduced throttle settings.
exam_tip: Reduced throttle = greater venturi pressure drop = greater temperature drop = faster ice accretion. At low throttle, carb icing risk is HIGH. At full throttle, risk is lower. Apply carb heat during descent and cruise, especially at reduced power. This is critical during reduced-power flight.
key_facts: Throttle setting dramatically affects carb icing rate
Reduced throttle = greater venturi pressure drop
Greater pressure drop = greater temperature drop (5-10°C or more)
Low throttle = faster ice accumulation
Full throttle = less pronounced cooling effect
Carb icing risk is HIGH during descent and cruise
Carb icing risk is LOWER at full power
Apply carb heat during reduced-power flight
Carb heat warms incoming air before carburettor
Essential protection during descent and low-power cruise

syllabus_desc: Explain the conditions that can cause carburettor icing while on the ground.
concept: You might think carburettor icing only happens in flight, but it can form on the ground too  -  and it's often overlooked. When you start your engine or run it for taxiing or ground checks, if conditions are right, carb icing can form inside the carburettor and restrict fuel-air flow, causing rough running or even engine failure on take-off. This is particularly dangerous if you discover it after you've committed to the take-off roll. On the ground, carb icing happens when the air temperature is in the dangerous range (-10 to +30°C, worst 0-20°C) and humidity is high. Post-rain conditions are the worst  -  the air has just been saturated with moisture as rain passes through. Coastal areas with high humidity and moderate temperatures are also vulnerable. Early morning after a clear, humid night is a classic scenario: the temperature is moderate, humidity is very high (air has cooled and moisture has condensed), and your carburettor is at risk. If you run your engine at low or idle power while parked or taxiing, the carburettor cooling effect is pronounced, and ice can form. This is why it's important to apply carb heat during ground operation if you suspect high-humidity conditions. If you suspect carb icing, you'll notice engine roughness, a drop in engine RPM, or difficulty maintaining power. If this happens on the ground, you should immediately apply full carb heat (or full engine heat if your system doesn't have a separate carb heat control). If it happens during take-off roll, declare an emergency and abort if safe to do so. After take-off, if you suspect carb icing in flight, apply carb heat immediately and descend if necessary to warmer air.
exam_tip: Carb icing on ground: post-rain conditions, high humidity, moderate temperature (0-20°C), especially at idle/low power. Early morning is dangerous. Apply carb heat during ground operation in suspect conditions. Engine roughness signals icing. Abort take-off if suspected. Apply full carb heat if engine affected.
key_facts: Carb icing can form on the ground as well as in flight
Dangerous conditions: post-rain, high humidity, moderate temperature
Temperature range: -10 to +30°C, worst 0-20°C
Early morning after clear, humid nights is classic scenario
Low or idle power operation increases risk
Running engine at taxi power in suspect conditions is risky
Engine roughness or RPM drop signals icing
Apply carb heat during ground operation if conditions suspect
If engine affected: apply full carb heat immediately
If on take-off roll: abort and declare emergency if safe

syllabus_desc: State the three conditions required for the development of thunderstorms.
concept: Thunderstorms are among the most dangerous weather phenomena you'll encounter. They don't just happen randomly  -  they require three specific atmospheric conditions to develop, and understanding these conditions helps you predict when storms are likely and how to avoid them. First condition: you need moisture. Thunderstorms require abundant water vapour in the lower atmosphere  -  the fuel that powers the convection. This is why thunderstorms are common in warm, humid environments: tropical regions, areas near the sea where evaporation is high, and areas downwind of warm water bodies. In NZ, moisture-laden air typically comes from north or northeast winds bringing tropical moisture, or from the Tasman Sea. Second condition: you need instability. The atmosphere must be unstable  -  meaning that if a parcel of air is forced upward, it will continue to rise on its own because it's warmer than its surroundings. This requires a steep lapse rate: temperature must decrease rapidly with altitude. If the atmosphere is stable, rising air cools, becomes denser than its surroundings, and sinks back down  -  no storm develops. Instability is usually present when cold air overlies warm, moist air near the surface. Third condition: you need a trigger or lifting mechanism. Even with moisture and instability, the atmosphere won't spontaneously develop a storm. Something has to force the air upward to start the convection. This trigger can be a cold front (mechanical lifting), solar heating on a warm day (orographic or thermal lifting), or hills and mountains (orographic lifting). In NZ, the Southern Alps provide a powerful orographic trigger. With all three conditions present  -  moisture, instability, and a trigger  -  thunderstorms will develop. Without all three, no storm.
exam_tip: Three conditions for thunderstorms: (1) Moisture (water vapour in lower atmosphere); (2) Instability (steep lapse rate, temperature decreases rapidly with height); (3) Trigger/lifting mechanism (cold front, orographic, thermal, or mountain). All three required.
key_facts: Three conditions essential: moisture, instability, and trigger
Condition 1  -  Moisture: abundant water vapour, tropical air, sea influence
Condition 2  -  Instability: steep lapse rate, cold air over warm moist air
Condition 3  -  Trigger: cold front, orographic, thermal, or mountain lift
NZ moisture sources: tropical north/northeast winds, Tasman Sea
Cold fronts provide mechanical lifting
Southern Alps provide orographic lifting
Solar heating on warm days provides thermal lifting
All three conditions must be present for storm development
Without all three, thunderstorms will not form

syllabus_desc: Describe the three stages in the life-cycle of a thunderstorm.
concept: Thunderstorms have a life cycle  -  they develop, mature, and dissipate. Understanding these stages helps you recognize where a storm is in its development and what hazards to expect. Stage One: Cumulus Stage (Development Stage). The storm starts as a towering cumulus cloud with powerful updrafts. Warm, moist air is rising, cooling as it ascends, and condensing into cloud. Inside this cloud, updrafts can exceed 5-10 metres per second (strong, but not yet extreme). There's little precipitation at this stage  -  most of the condensed water is still suspended aloft in droplets. There's no rain yet, or only light rain. The cloud top is still growing vertically, reaching higher into the atmosphere. Visually, you see a towering, cauliflower-like cloud with a sharp upper edge. Stage Two: Mature Stage (Maximum Intensity). The updrafts reach their peak strength (sometimes exceeding 15-20 metres per second), and now the cloud contains a mix of updrafts and downdrafts. The downdrafts are caused by precipitation loading  -  raindrops, hail, and ice particles are heavy, they fall, and they drag air downward with them. This is when the storm is most violent. Heavy rain, hail, and lightning are at their maximum. The cloud is typically anvil-shaped or mushroom-shaped at the top (the anvil marks the upper boundary of the storm). The cloud base darkens considerably. This is the most dangerous stage for aircraft  -  there's wind shear where updrafts and downdrafts meet, severe turbulence, heavy rain/hail, lightning, and ice-laden conditions. Stage Three: Dissipating Stage (Decay Stage). Precipitation is heavier than it was, and the downdrafts dominate. The updrafts weaken and finally cease. Cooling air from the downdrafts spreads out at the surface, cutting off the warm, moist air supply that feeds the storm. The storm weakens, the rain diminishes, and the cloud gradually spreads out and loses its vertical development. Visually, the sharp, towering profile is replaced by a more diffuse cloud. Eventually, the storm dissipates entirely  -  all that's left is weak precipitation and possibly some gusty wind at the surface as the downdrafts spread out.
exam_tip: Three stages: (1) Cumulus  -  updrafts only, light rain, towering; (2) Mature  -  updrafts AND downdrafts, heaviest rain and hail, most dangerous, anvil-shaped; (3) Dissipating  -  downdrafts dominate, rain weakens, vertical profile collapses. Remember: Updrafts early → Strong mix middle → Downdrafts late.

Heavy rain, hail, and lightning at maximum
Wind shear and severe turbulence
Anvil-shaped or mushroom-shaped top
Darkened cloud base
Most dangerous stage for aircraft

syllabus_desc: List the hazards associated with thunderstorms.
concept: Thunderstorms present multiple hazards, any one of which can be fatal to a light aircraft. Here's the full list. Severe Turbulence: Wind shear from the collision of updrafts and downdrafts creates severe, unpredictable turbulence. The aircraft can experience sudden, violent altitude changes and structural stress. Wind shear can also occur as you transition from the storm's inflow to its outflow, with wind direction reversals. Heavy Precipitation and Hail: Rain reduces visibility and can clog engine air intakes. Hail causes mechanical damage  -  it pits windscreens, dents fuselage, and damages engine components. Lightning: Direct lightning strikes are rare (aircraft structure provides some protection through bonding), but lightning can cause electrical damage, especially to avionics and instrument systems. Lightning also temporarily blinds you with its flash, reducing visibility and situational awareness. Icing: Cumulonimbus clouds contain abundant supercooled water droplets above the freezing level. Icing rates in severe storms are extremely high, and ice can accumulate faster than any de-icing system can handle. This is the most serious icing environment you can encounter. Microbursts and Wind Shear: Localized downdrafts (microbursts) can descend at speeds exceeding 5-10 metres per second, creating a localized strong downward wind that can overwhelm an aircraft's climb capability, especially near the surface during landing or take-off. Reduced Visibility: Heavy rain, hail, and suspended water reduce visibility to near zero in severe storms. Disorientation: The combination of turbulence, wind shear, and zero visibility can cause disorientation and vertigo, especially if you're in IMC (Instrument Meteorological Conditions) and can't see the horizon. Updraft Hazard: Powerful updrafts can carry an aircraft to extreme altitudes, beyond its design limits, especially if you attempt to climb out through the storm. Reduced Engine Power: Hail ingestion, water ingestion, and icing of engine induction systems can cause rapid power loss. Every one of these hazards is serious enough on its own. Combined, they make thunderstorms the most dangerous atmospheric phenomenon for light aircraft.
exam_tip: Major thunderstorm hazards: severe turbulence and wind shear, heavy precipitation and hail, lightning, severe icing (most dangerous icing environment), microbursts (dangerous near surface), reduced visibility, disorientation, extreme altitude from powerful updrafts, engine power loss. Never fly through or near thunderstorms.

Icing rates exceed de-ice system capability

Microbursts dangerous near surface during landing/take-off
Visibility reduced to near zero in severe storms
Disorientation from turbulence, wind shear, and zero visibility
Powerful updrafts can carry aircraft to extreme altitudes
Hail ingestion causes rapid engine power loss

syllabus_desc: Explain why light aircraft should always avoid flight in the vicinity of thunderstorms.
concept: The fundamental rule is absolute: light aircraft must always avoid flight in the vicinity of thunderstorms. Here's why. First, light aircraft simply don't have the performance to escape a thunderstorm once you're in or near one. A typical single-engine aircraft has a climb rate of 300-500 feet per minute. A thunderstorm updraft can exceed 15-20 metres per second (that's 3,000-4,000 feet per minute). You cannot outclimb a thunderstorm. Second, you have no protection. Commercial aircraft have specialized weather radar to detect storms and navigate around them. They have de-icing and lightning-protection systems, and they're built to operate in difficult weather. Light aircraft have none of this. Your only defence is avoidance. Third, thunderstorm hazards combine into a lethal package. You might survive one hazard in isolation  -  severe turbulence, or icing, or wind shear  -  but the combination of all of them simultaneously is overwhelming. The turbulence alone can throw an unprepared pilot around the cockpit or cause structural failure. The icing exceeds any de-ice capability you might have. The wind shear can cause sudden loss of altitude from which you can't recover. Fourth, conditions deteriorate instantly and unpredictably. Thunderstorms develop and intensify over minutes. A visual forecast showing scattered storms can evolve into a severe squall line before you cross the aerodrome boundary. You can't outrun it; you can't climb over it; you can't predict exactly where the worst conditions will be. Your only reliable strategy is distance avoidance. The standard is to maintain at least 8 nautical miles horizontal distance from visible storm clouds  -  but in NZ conditions, with fast-developing mountain-triggered storms, even more distance is advisable. If a storm develops on your route, don't try to navigate between cells. Divert, turn back, or find an alternate aerodrome. It's that simple. No time saving is worth the risk.
exam_tip: Light aircraft cannot outclimb thunderstorms (updrafts exceed 3,000+ fpm; typical light aircraft climb 300-500 fpm). No de-ice or radar protection. Hazards combine into lethal package. Conditions deteriorate instantly. Maintain minimum 8 NM distance from storm clouds. Divert if storms develop on route. Avoidance is the only strategy.

syllabus_desc: Define the Föhn wind.
concept: The Föhn wind is a distinctive, warm, dry downslope wind that occurs on the lee side (downwind side) of mountain ranges when air is forced to flow over high terrain. In New Zealand, the most famous example is the Canterbury nor'wester  -  a Föhn wind that brings warm, dry conditions to the eastern side of the Southern Alps while the western side experiences wet conditions. Here's how it works: moist air on the windward side of the mountains is forced upward by the topography. As it rises, it cools at the moist adiabatic lapse rate (slower cooling because condensation releases latent heat). Precipitation falls out on the windward side, removing moisture from the air mass. As the now-drier air descends on the lee side, it compresses and warms at the dry adiabatic lapse rate (faster warming because it's dry). The result is warm, dry air descending on the lee side. The Canterbury nor'wester is the classic NZ example: moist air flows from the west across the Southern Alps, loses its moisture to rain on the western slopes, and then descends onto the Canterbury Plains as warm, dry, northwesterly wind. Temperature increases, humidity drops dramatically, and visibility becomes excellent  -  but the winds can be powerful, and the rapid transition from wet to dry conditions can fool unsuspecting pilots. Föhn winds are significant for aviation because they're warm, which can affect your aircraft's density altitude performance, and they're often strong and turbulent. They're also associated with rapid weather changes and a risk of mountain waves and rotor zones on the lee side.
exam_tip: Föhn wind: warm, dry downslope wind on the lee side of mountains. Moist air rises on windward side, loses moisture to precipitation, descends as warm, dry air on lee side. NZ example: Canterbury nor'wester. High temperature, low humidity, excellent visibility, but often strong and turbulent.
key_facts: Föhn wind: warm, dry downslope wind on lee side of mountains
Caused by forced lifting of moist air over mountains
Windward side: air rises, cools, loses moisture to precipitation
Lee side: dry air descends, compresses, and warms
NZ example: Canterbury nor'wester across Southern Alps
Characteristics: warm temperature, low humidity, excellent visibility
Wind is often strong and turbulent
Rapid transition from wet (windward) to dry (lee)
Associated with mountain waves and rotor zones
Affects aircraft density altitude performance

syllabus_desc: In Föhn wind conditions, describe the typical weather: (a) to windward of the mountain range; (b) above the mountain range; (c) on the lee side of the mountain range.
concept: In Föhn wind conditions, weather patterns differ dramatically across the mountain range  -  three distinct zones with very different characteristics. To Windward (Upwind Side) of the Mountain Range: The air is moist, driven by the Föhn flow toward the mountains. Cloud develops as the air is forced upward and cools. You'll typically see low cloud, stratus, or stratocumulus covering the windward slopes. Visibility is often poor due to the cloud and precipitation. Precipitation (rain or drizzle) is common on the windward slopes and foothills. The wind is typically steady, aligned with the Föhn flow direction. In the Canterbury nor'wester example, the windward side (west of the Southern Alps) experiences wet conditions with low cloud. Above the Mountain Range (Mountain Crest and Above): Above the crest, the air is transitional. Cloud is often present, particularly where the air is still rising. But the air is progressively drier as it crosses the crest, so cloud density decreases as you move toward the lee side of the crest. Temperature is lower at altitude. Wind speed typically increases with height, and the wind aligns with the upper-level flow direction (often the same as the surface Föhn wind direction). You may encounter lenticular clouds (lens-shaped standing waves) above the lee side of the crest if wind speed is sufficient. On the Lee Side of the Mountain Range: This is where the Föhn characteristics are most pronounced. The descending air compresses and warms. Cloud clears dramatically  -  often broken or few cloud layers, with good visibility. Humidity drops sharply. Temperature is noticeably warmer than on the windward side at the same altitude. Wind is typically strong and steady, aligned with the Föhn direction (nor'west in the Canterbury case). The contrast is striking: one side has wet, cloudy, low-visibility conditions; the other side has warm, clear, windy conditions.
exam_tip: Föhn zones: (a) Windward  -  moist air, cloud, poor visibility, precipitation on slopes; (b) Above crest  -  transitional, lenticular clouds may form; (c) Lee side  -  warm, dry, excellent visibility, strong wind, dramatic contrast with windward side.
key_facts: Windward zone: moist air, cloud development, poor visibility
Windward: stratus/stratocumulus cloud on slopes
Windward: precipitation (rain/drizzle) common
Windward: steady wind aligned with Föhn flow
Above crest: transitional conditions, cloud often present
Above crest: air progressively drier approaching lee side
Above crest: wind speed typically increases with height
Above crest: lenticular clouds possible if wind sufficient
Lee side: descending air warms and compresses
Lee side: cloud clears, few/broken layers
Lee side: excellent visibility, low humidity
Lee side: temperature warmer than windward side
Lee side: strong, steady wind in Föhn direction
Dramatic contrast across mountain range

syllabus_desc: Describe the mountain lee-wave (standing wave) development process.
concept: Mountain lee-waves (standing waves) form when strong wind flows perpendicular to a mountain range, and the airflow patterns create stationary wave patterns on the lee side. Here's how it develops. When stable, moist air flows toward a mountain range, it's forced upward by the topography. As the air flows up the windward slope, it rises, cools, and compresses. At the crest, it starts to descend on the lee side. But here's the key: because the air is stable, it doesn't just smoothly descend. Instead, it overshoots the descent and oscillates  -  like a pendulum  -  on the lee side. This oscillation creates a series of standing waves: the air rises in the first wave trough (and can be forced high into the atmosphere), descends in the first wave crest, rises again in a second trough further downwind, and so on. The waves remain stationary relative to the terrain (they don't move downstream with the wind)  -  hence 'standing' waves. The waves are typically spaced 5-15 kilometres apart, depending on wind speed and atmosphere stability. Several conditions must be present for strong lee-wave development. First, wind must flow roughly perpendicular to the mountain range (not parallel to it). Second, the wind must be strong  -  typically 20+ knots at altitude. Third, the lower atmosphere must be stable (warm layer aloft or inversion layer). Fourth, the upper atmosphere must be unstable or neutral so the wave can propagate upward. In the Southern Alps of NZ, lee-waves form regularly when strong northwest winds flow perpendicular to the range. The waves can extend far downwind (30+ kilometres or more) and can reach very high altitudes. Lenticular clouds often mark the wave crests, particularly where moisture is available to form visible clouds. Rotor zones (powerful circular vortices) can form beneath the lee-wave crests, with extreme turbulence.
exam_tip: Lee-wave formation: stable air forced over mountain range oscillates on lee side. Creates standing wave pattern (stationary relative to terrain). Requires perpendicular wind, strong wind (20+ knots), stable lower atmosphere, unstable upper atmosphere. NZ Southern Alps: strong NW winds create lee-waves. Lenticular clouds mark wave crests. Rotor zones form beneath.
key_facts: Lee-wave: standing wave pattern formed on lee side of mountains
Stable air forced over mountains oscillates, creating wave pattern
Waves stationary relative to terrain (don't move downstream)
Wave spacing: 5-15 km, depends on wind speed and stability
Condition 1: wind perpendicular to mountain range
Condition 2: strong wind (typically 20+ knots at altitude)
Condition 3: stable lower atmosphere
Condition 4: unstable/neutral upper atmosphere
NZ Southern Alps: common with strong NW winds
Waves can extend far downwind (30+ km or more)
Waves can reach extreme altitudes
Lenticular clouds mark wave crests (where moisture present)
Rotor zones form beneath wave crests with extreme turbulence

syllabus_desc: Describe the formation of rotor zones.
concept: Rotor zones are areas of intense, chaotic turbulence that form on the lee side of mountains, typically beneath or behind the standing lee-waves. They're among the most dangerous wind phenomena for aviation, and understanding their formation is critical for mountain flying. Here's what happens. When stable air is forced up and over a mountain range, it creates the lee-wave pattern we discussed. But the airflow doesn't just smoothly oscillate  -  it creates circular vortices or rotors beneath the wave crests. These form because of wind shear: the wind speed increases with height, and the air in the lower atmosphere (below the wave crest level) rotates in response to this wind shear gradient. The rotor is essentially a large rotating vortex  -  air rotating in a circular pattern, often in a horizontal axis (rolling). The rotor is most intense directly beneath or slightly downwind of the lee-wave crest, and typically extends from the surface up to somewhere near the wave crest level. The vertical extent of the rotor depends on the strength of the wind shear and the wave amplitude, but rotors are typically 1,000-2,000 feet deep or sometimes deeper. Critically, rotor zones are often obscured by rotor clouds  -  turbulent, ragged clouds that form as moist air circulates in the rotor. These clouds can be deceptive: they might look relatively harmless, but inside is violent, unpredictable turbulence. You can't reliably see the rotor from the air or ground  -  it's the rotating air itself, not a distinct visual feature. Rotor intensity increases with wind speed and atmospheric stability. Strong winds and steep, stable layers create intense rotors. In NZ, when strong northwest winds flow across the Southern Alps, severe rotor zones develop on the lee side, often extending far downwind of the mountains.
exam_tip: Rotor zones: intense turbulence beneath lee-waves, caused by wind shear and circular air rotation. Rotor is rotating vortex (horizontal axis). Typically 1,000-2,000 ft deep. Often obscured by rotor clouds (ragged, turbulent). Can't see rotor from air/ground  -  it's rotating air. Intensity increases with wind speed and stability.
key_facts: Rotor zones: intense turbulence beneath lee-waves on lee side
Caused by wind shear and circular air rotation
Rotor is a horizontal-axis rotating vortex
Forms directly beneath or slightly downwind of wave crest
Extends from surface to near wave crest level
Typical depth: 1,000-2,000 feet (can be deeper)
Often obscured by rotor clouds (ragged, turbulent clouds)
Rotor clouds form as moist air circulates in rotor
Rotor clouds can be deceptive  -  look harmless but violent inside
Rotor itself is not a distinct visual feature
Intensity increases with wind speed
Intensity increases with atmospheric stability
Strong NW winds across Southern Alps create severe rotors
Rotors can extend far downwind of mountains

syllabus_desc: Explain the associated dangers of rotor zones to aircraft operations.
concept: Rotor zones present extreme dangers to aircraft, especially light aircraft. The turbulence is severe, unpredictable, and can exceed the structural limits of light aircraft. Here are the specific dangers. Severe Turbulence: Turbulence in rotor zones is moderate to severe, and sometimes extreme. The rotating air creates sudden vertical wind shears and up- and down-drafts. An aircraft entering a rotor can experience sudden, violent altitude changes  -  hundreds of feet per minute in either direction. The turbulence is chaotic and non-repetitive, making it difficult to anticipate or control. Structural Stress and Damage: The violent movements can stress the aircraft structure beyond design limits. Light aircraft are particularly vulnerable because they're not built to withstand extreme turbulence. Severe turbulence can cause structural failure of wings or tail surfaces, or can cause flutter (oscillating failure) of control surfaces. Pilot Disorientation and Loss of Control: Severe turbulence can disorient pilots, especially if transitioning into IMC (Instrument Meteorological Conditions). The violent movements throw the pilot around the cockpit, making it difficult to maintain control of the aircraft or read instruments. Loss of situational awareness is common. Inability to Climb or Descend: The chaotic vertical winds can prevent the aircraft from climbing or descending as commanded. You might be trying to climb out of the rotor, but the downdrafts are stronger than your climb rate, trapping you in the rotor zone. Conversely, you might be descending too fast (driven down by downdrafts) and unable to stop. Induced Stall and Spin Risk: The violent turbulence can cause sudden stalls or approach-to-stall conditions. In a rotor zone, a stall is extremely dangerous because recovery is difficult in the chaotic airflow, and you might be forced down by downdrafts faster than you can recover. Accident Risk: Rotor zones have been responsible for numerous accidents and fatalities, particularly in mountainous regions. Aircraft have been structurally damaged or destroyed by rotor turbulence. Pilots have been unable to escape rotor zones, leading to controlled flight into terrain (CFIT) or structural failure. The danger is real and documented. Avoidance is the only strategy. Never intentionally fly through rotor zones. If you encounter severe turbulence in a mountain area that you suspect is a rotor, exit immediately  -  descend to very low level (below the rotor layer), fly upwind (away from the lee side), or climb above the rotor layer if altitude and aircraft performance permit. Most importantly, plan your mountain flights to avoid the conditions that create rotors: avoid flying on the lee side of mountains when strong winds are forecast.
exam_tip: Rotor zone dangers: severe turbulence (chaotic, non-repetitive); structural stress and potential damage; pilot disorientation; inability to climb/descend (trapped by downdrafts); stall and spin risk; significant accident risk. Light aircraft particularly vulnerable. Avoidance essential. If encountered, exit immediately.
key_facts: Rotor turbulence is severe, chaotic, and non-repetitive
Sudden vertical wind shears and up/down-drafts
Violent altitude changes (hundreds of feet per minute)
Structural stress can exceed aircraft design limits
Light aircraft particularly vulnerable to structural failure
Risk of wing/tail failure or control surface flutter
Pilot disorientation common, especially in turbulence/IMC
Loss of situational awareness likely
Difficulty maintaining aircraft control
Aircraft can be trapped in rotor by downdrafts
Unable to climb out despite maximum power
Unable to stop descent (driven down by downdrafts)
Sudden stalls and approach-to-stall conditions
Stall recovery extremely difficult in chaotic airflow
CFIT risk and structural failure documented
Never intentionally fly through rotor zones
If encountered: exit immediately (descend below, climb above, or fly upwind)

syllabus_desc: With regard to VFR flight in a light aircraft in mountainous terrain, describe the meteorological factors that should be considered during the flight planning phase and en-route, including: (a) cloud base; (b) turbulence; (c) adverse and favourable winds; (d) visibility; (e) track selection; (f) the anticipated timing of any expected weather change.
concept: Flying VFR in mountainous terrain requires careful consideration of multiple meteorological factors  -  both before you depart and continuously during flight. Let me walk you through each one. (a) Cloud Base: Cloud base is critical because VFR flight requires you to maintain visual reference. If cloud base is below the height of terrain features on your route, you can't fly over the mountains at safe altitude and maintain visual reference. In NZ, you need to consider terrain elevation on your planned route. Check the ARFORs and TAFs for forecast cloud levels. If the cloud base is forecast to drop below your planned cruise altitude or below the terrain height + required safety margin, the flight is not viable VFR. Cloud base can also lower en-route as conditions deteriorate, so plan conservatively. Always have an alternate aerodrome where you can descend if cloud lowers. (b) Turbulence: Turbulence in mountains is predictable and avoidable if you plan correctly. Turbulence is most severe when strong winds blow perpendicular to high terrain. Check the wind forecast for your area. NZ ARFORs typically describe turbulence levels (light, moderate, severe). Avoid flying on the lee side of mountains when strong winds are forecast. Thermal turbulence is common during the day when mountains are heated by the sun. Early morning is typically smoother. Rotor zones form with strong winds and stable atmosphere. Wind shear is most severe near the surface, so flying higher generally avoids the worst shear, but very high altitude flight can put you in severe rotor zones above standing waves. (c) Adverse and Favourable Winds: Always check the wind forecast at your cruise altitude and along your route. If a strong headwind is forecast, your fuel endurance is reduced and your trip might not be feasible. If a strong tailwind is available, it's advantageous, but don't rely on it to extend your range. In mountainous terrain, local wind patterns are important. Valleys often have wind directions aligned with the valley axis. Downslope and upslope winds form depending on time of day and heating. Understand the wind pattern and use favourable winds when possible; avoid flying into strong adverse winds. (d) Visibility: Visibility in mountainous areas can be excellent or abysmal, depending on weather. Good visibility is essential for VFR flight  -  you need to see mountains and other aircraft. Check forecasts for visibility. Moisture, precipitation, and cloud can reduce visibility. Rain can reduce visibility to a few hundred metres. Check satellite and radar for precipitation near your route. Coastal areas and windward slopes often have lower visibility due to moisture. Lee sides typically have excellent visibility in Föhn conditions. Plan routes where visibility is expected to be best. (e) Track Selection: Choose your route carefully in mountains. Avoid flying directly toward high terrain that might trap you in a narrow valley if weather deteriorates. Fly along valleys where terrain slopes away on at least one side, so you have an escape route if you need to turn back. Avoid flying with terrain at your wing level  -  you want terrain visible beneath you. Fly high enough to clear terrain with a comfortable safety margin (typically 2,000 feet above terrain is a good minimum for light aircraft). Follow valleys downslope if you need to descend (gravity helps your descent rate). Always know your escape routes  -  how you'd turn back or exit if weather closed in. (f) Anticipated Timing of Weather Changes: Weather in mountains can change rapidly. Check the TAF and ARFOR validity periods. If the forecast shows deterioration (cloud lowering, wind increasing) at a particular time, ensure your flight completes before that time. Fronts can pass rapidly, bringing sudden changes in wind, cloud, and visibility. Thermal activity is strongest in the afternoon. Early morning flights are typically smoother and more stable. Plan your flight timing to avoid the worst forecast weather window. Check the timing of any significant wind changes or frontal passages. In NZ, afternoon heating can trigger thunderstorm development  -  avoid flying in mountains in late afternoon in summer if thunderstorm development is possible. All of these factors tie together: choose a route with good visibility and favourable terrain, plan your flight timing to avoid adverse winds and deteriorating weather, maintain a safe altitude above terrain, and always know your escape routes and alternate aerodromes.
exam_tip: (a) Cloud base must allow VFR flight at safe altitude above terrain; (b) Avoid lee-side turbulence, thermal turbulence (afternoon worst), rotor zones; (c) Check wind forecast; adverse headwinds reduce fuel endurance; (d) Visibility essential for VFR; check for precipitation; (e) Choose safe valley routes with escape routes; fly above terrain with safety margin; (f) Plan flight timing to avoid forecast weather deterioration; know frontal passage timing; avoid afternoon heating.
